I've bounced off every other extraction shooter that I've tried. This one is special though. The settings, art direction, aethetics, story, and music make for a very unique shooter experience. The online narrative around this game is that it's only for hardcore players. That's something that's true for the entire genre though and I think that Marathon does an amazing job of providing value and legitimately fuin and tense gameplay for those of us that aren't nearly as good at multiplayer fps games and extraction shooters.    I'm also terrible at multiplayer shooters, but the game is really well designed to keep me engaged with the AI enemies. They really pack a bunch and aren't just random mobs to obliterate. They're real encounters that require thinking or else you'll be ioverwhelmed. I also really appreciate that the objectives for 5/6 companies aren't even PvP focused and usually don't require you to extract. I feel like I actually make progress on my character and story even if I die in the round. They even give you free basic kits to go into the rounds with so that if you don't extract, you really haven't lost anything cause you didn't go in with anything in the first place.    Definfitely worth the money. I hope that Bungie will do other F2P weeks in the future to help introduce even more people.

Pretty cool game... made for the smallest audience possible.  It's a PvP only, Extraction shooter, live service game, that cost 40€, and looks like a pharmacy.  Some reasons why the game have a very niche audience:   -It's PvP only (for now), so a lot of people who were excited for a new Marathon (including me) got disappointed when it was shown to be an extraction shooter, and felt like a trendy cash-in.   -Most people who want to play Extraction shooters already have their "main", from Hunt to Tarkov. And it's already kind of a niche genre.   -It's 40€ (base), for 4 maps (Basically 3 if you play Solo), no solo campaign, and a Live service game (aka "Might disappear in 6 months if Sony get bored") which might put off some people.   -Game looks great, but that won't attract the majority of people who play PvP games like Tarkov and CoD with that aesthetic.   -The TTK (Time-to-kill) is ridiculously low. You die in a split second. Most casual PvP players will not stay past a few hours from dying over and over and go back to their usual game.   -The game was conceived for 3-man teams. So most of the content is balanced around it, including some quests. If you don't have friends who want to join you, and you don't like a harder Solo experience than other Extraction shooters, you might be out of luck.   -The game doesn't run well on lower end computer (Like mine). The System requirement are even a complete lie, i am slightly above those and i still lose frames pretty hard with everything at minimum.  (The positive quickly for fairness: Really cool art style, shoot well, sound good, very cool story bits)  So that's a lot of filters, and why you probably see a rather low playerbase. The players are dedicated though, and Bungie got plans (Adding more PvE oriented modes, more story next season, etc) And as i said, game is actually pretty cool so i recommend... if you are the niche audience Bungie aimed for (intentionally or not).
Long Live Marathon and Destiny

As someone who's played a variety of extraction shooters, in terms of difficulty I'd say the learning curve of this game is a lot less steeper than something like Tarkov, but only slightly bigger than ARC Raiders. The Shells add a dash of hero shooter to the mix, but the use of Cores, Implants, and the new Cradle system gives you plenty of options to upgrade and tailor your Runner to fit your playstyle.   Some might be scared away by the threat of losing everything you're carrying if you die mid-Run, which is made worse by the lack of a safe pocket like ARC: Raiders or Escape from Tarkov. But this game does do a good job at giving you options of pre-built kits, both free and bought for a few thousand credits, which rotate every day between the different factions that sponsor them. Between that and the Rook Shell, a Shell used exclusively to scavenge for loot in a solo Run with a pre-made free loadout, you have plenty of options if your stash is running empty, or if you want to run a Contract without putting your own gear at risk.   Personally, I've had a blast playing this game this season. I've been playing this over ARC Raiders because of the satisfying gunplay, and the fact that I can engage in PvP without feeling guilty afterwards. That being said, there are Mercy Kits in this game that allow you to revive enemy Runners, and I did manage to convince someone who downed me to pick me back up. In other words, friendly interactions are possible, if rare.

Man, this game is great. I was drawn to it by its visual style and lore when first announced but was very reluctant about whether or not I, someone who had put aside any and all kinds of PvP gaming for almost a decade, would enjoy something like an extraction shooter. The free week was a perfect opportunity to see what it was all about and to be honest, I am baffled by how great this game is and how people are blindly hating and wanting it to fail.    Most of the time, you'll be playing this game as a PvE game with the occasional enemy player encounter and it's mostly a game of cat and mouse, sneak around, do the stuff you have to while not drawing attention of other players - or just go around listening for your prey. While yes, it sucks to die and lose your gear, the more you play, the more you learn how to tackle player encounters (or just plainly avoid them if you wish to). Apparently, this season introduced a change at how quickly you earn better items. In just under 10 hours I have a full vault with a few interesting weapons I enjoyed playing with, a bunch of shields and backpacks. If I WANT to risk my stuff, I have enough left to not make me feel like ♥♥♥♥ if I get jumped by someone else (or die to the enemy bots, which has happened quite a lot to me). If I don't want to, I can just use the free "sponsored kit" of the day or buy a slightly better kit. So yeah, losing items ain't that big of a fuss, and the rush of dopamine from eliminating other players is great.    What really got me though was the ominous atmosphere and how immersive it all feels. The visuals are great, sort of a post-cyberpunk world where nothing really matters since you're just literally shells on a failed colony. The sound design is out of this world and the soundtrack is incredible. When playing solo, it almost feels like a horror game - not to mention the Night map which is just bonkers.    Honestly, if you are on the fence about this one because you think extraction shooters are not for you, give it a shot during a free week (I'm sure more will come). You might be surprised.

415 hours in and I’m still queueing for “one more run”.  Marathon is not perfect. The criticism around server issues, bugs, confusing UI/UX, poor onboarding, long matchmaking times, and new players bouncing off is valid. This is not the easiest game to get into, and I completely understand why some people try it for a few hours and decide it isn’t for them. That said, once Marathon clicks, it really clicks.  The gunplay is excellent, the maps are brilliantly designed, and the extraction loop is incredibly addictive. Even after a bad loss, I usually find myself immediately queueing again because the next run could be the one. There is a constant sense of risk, reward, and momentum that very few games manage to capture this well.  I mostly play in a trio, and that is where the game shines. Team coordination, choosing the right shell, deciding when to push or extract, and recovering from chaotic fights all make every run feel different. The shells also do a great job of giving each player a clear identity without turning the game into a full hero shooter. I’ve mostly played Recon and Triage so far, and I’m looking forward to trying Sentinel properly now that Season 2 has launched.  The endgame is also a huge reason I keep coming back. Cryo Archive and the Compiler boss fight give the game something substantial to build towards, rather than just being an endless loop of looting and leaving. That matters a lot. For comparison, the only other extraction shooter I’ve really played is ARC Raiders. I have over 200 hours in that, but I haven’t touched it since Marathon launched. For me, Marathon is simply the better game, especially because it has actual endgame-level content to chase.  Would I recommend it to everyone? No. If you hate extraction shooters, dislike losing gear, or need a game to be instantly welcoming, this may not be for you. It needs polish, the new player experience needs work, and some of the common complaints are completely fair.  But if you like tense PvPvE, strong gunplay, meaningful team play, proper endgame goals, and that “just one more run” feeling, ignore the hate and try it.

Came in expecting another extraction shooter and honestly ended up having more fun than I thought.  Bungie's gunplay is still elite. Every weapon feels satisfying to use and fights are intense enough to keep me queueing for one more run. The art style also deserves credit because it gives the game its own identity instead of looking like every other sci-fi shooter out there.  It's not perfect though. Progression feels a little thin right now and after a while some matches start blending together. If you already hate extraction shooters, this game probably won't change your mind.  But if you're here for good FPS gameplay, Marathon delivers. There are definitely things that need work, but the core gameplay is strong enough to keep me interested.  Pros  Excellent gunplay Smooth movement Great art direction Intense firefights  Cons  Progression lacks depth Repetitive after extended play Limited appeal outside the extraction shooter audience Needs more content and variety  Rating: 7.5/10  Worth recommending, but there's still room for Bungie to build on a strong foundation.

This game certainly has its issues other than the ones intrinsic to the genre, but you as a thinking adult can see the vitriol of somebody posting steam charts and pointing and screaming that the game is woke and transgender and realize that those people aren't talking about the game. Marathon didn't kill Destiny 2 either, this was clearly a side project that they now have to bank on because of a CEO golden parachuting himself out during an acquisition. You can use your eyes and brain and know these things. Right?  On a technical level, Marathon is currently struggling. It runs alright on my system but load times and networking are both abysmal by the standards of a company as large as Bungie. Certain basic QOL elements such as multi-loot-selection have only recently been added, and there's a bunch of micro-issues like that that will quickly add to your frustration when you're having a bad string of runs. This and balance are my biggest concerns going forward, Bungie is constantly making radical changes which will instantly date this review if I mention anything. Some of these are stark improvements, some are annoying, but they happen so regularly it can be tough to parse if you're not playing this as your main or only game.   I still find this game incredibly fun. The gunplay and sound design elements combine into a game that feels like you're rewarded both for skulking around patiently as well as for pushing the advantage and trying to rip another runner's gear right out of their arms. I find myself not enjoying random group queues at all but even solo play can lead to encountering others that you might want to team up with. Some of the newer PVE content is quite solid and feels rewarding on its own level, but nothing spikes your dopamine like winning a fight you weren't supposed to against another player or getting genuine relief that someone has decided that you do not die today.

[h1][b]LOVE THIS GAME SO MUCH BUT HAS IT'S OWN QUIRKS MOSTLY TECHNICAL[/b][/h1]    I've been playing this game since launch and still not done with it, gonna keep this review concise so adding points below-    [b]The Good Parts-[/b]  1. The environmental lighting and atmosphere in this game is phenomenal and perfect and nothing like I've seen anywhere.  2. The gunplay is really good but yeah I still suck at PvP😪.  3. The world and the artstyle is why specifically I got the game in first place and the animations and character designs are chef's kiss.  4. The enemy teams can be fighting hard but that's okay they're trying to keep their loot safe, but never met a toxic team, could be just me though. People in my lobbies always seemed nice and chill.  5. The UESC bots are really good but they upped their difficulty a bit so they are a bit of pain in the a** this season.  6. Love the new Nightmarsh map, especially the sponsored Nightmarsh (consider this mode a PvE mode) and saved up a ton of high tier loot by just grinding few runs.    [b]The Bad Parts-[/b]  1. Based on the specs I have I really don't expect my games to run at 70-50 FPS. It's been a widely complained problem in this game since launch and they still didn't fix the issue, it seems the game isn't even using my GPU at a moderate extent. Also changing graphics settings almost does nothing as if it's all same.  2. Voicechat connection issue is more than any game I have seen. I like to use VC and Prox chat a lot but 70% of the day it would show me that it's detecting my mic in game but others and my friend won't be able to hear me and same thing with them they talk I cannot hear them. Or I can hear them but they cannot hear me. It's still a mess. The text chat always works though.  3. The ping issue is still a thing and in these type of games ping matters a lot to me, I use a Wi-Fi with good and consistent speed and the router is sitting right next to my table, but the ping jumps happen time to time it's subtle but matters in gunfights. And yes I tried with a CAT6 ethernet cable too, it was same.  4. If I change the network when the game is on it starts to pop the connecting to Marathon servers text time to time and shows different network issues, the only solution then is to close and restart the game again.  5. Based on the matches I played it doesn't feel like there's any skill based matchmaking, the skill gaps are so high that it's preferable for me to just avoid if I notice a team, It's kinda unfair but don't know could be just a me problem.    **Bungieeeee please fix your damn technical issues :( **

The art style may be polarizing but I think it looks sick and unique in comparison to all these other shooters going for realism.  Lose your gear fear. Thats it. I just think the moment to moment gun fights with the even the current maps are really fun. Seeing how they mixed things up with Dire Marsh Night variation has me excited for the future if they keep at it.   Main criticisms so far is that while Bungie is listening the balance decisions while good seems like theres oversights. Not enough PvE in my PvEvP, I want the world of Tau Ceti to be deadly to me and other players instead of checklists and essentially alarms for the real threat of players. I can only say to keep at it and once you learn a bit more of the game enough to know how to take fights, know the maps and what to grab the game really is compelling.

PROS:  (1) Gameplay feels great.  (2) Combat feels great.  (3) Good audio (4) OKAY graphics when running around the MAPS they have made. Too many bright colors even there (I'll get into menus and UI later under cons), which makes it hard for me to identify what's going on, but it's generally pretty in the overworld. (5) Interesting upgrade choices and classes that make your style feel fun and keeps you addicted. (6) Good amount of free gear they provide on leveling.  CONS:  (1) I can honestly say this is the UGLIEST game UI I've ever seen. The color and design choice of the UI is atrocious. Too bright/neon, too harsh, text too hard to read. I get a headache almost immediately and have to look away for a few seconds every few minutes. I understand what theme they were trying to go for... that cyberpunk+retro look of menus, hackers, etc... but this was possibly the worst way to have done it. (2) Already overrun by sweaty players. Not sure how many cheaters are running the game into the ground, but holy smokes this game probably isn't for casual players. (3) Lack of detailed post-battle information (4) Lack of a training range (5) Not enough players to consistently keep duo queue up  In my opinion, the UI and text choices are chasing away A LOT of people. I've never had this issue with the plenty of games I play, so I do not think this is just a "me" thing. If they want to save this game, which I think is a good game underneath, they are going to have to sit down and acknowledge the problems, and have a visual redesign.

YES, I am a marathon runner now.  Goes without saying, but Bungie absolutely cooks when it comes to FPS games. This game shows how much the studio has learned from the past too.  Almost 70 hours into the aesthetically pleasing work of art. The music, the map design, the characters, all of it perfect.  Marathon takes excellent gunplay and teamwork coordination (when you run with crews) and turns up the CHAOS dial to maximum (try not to get 3rd partied). I truly appreciate the TTK in this game. Reminiscent of Halo to me. Give you plenty of time to decide you next move in the fight or run. There is an incredibly high skill ceiling in this game with a BIG learning curve (all the items, contracts, factions, codex, ect.). Luckily there is plenty enough resources in-game and many youtubers make guides & tips on how to play.  The variety of playstyles you will see with different shells adds just enough spice to the mix that I can easily see dumping 1000's of hours into this great game.  The addicting run>kill>loot loop has me hooked. I have also convinced 4 IRL to buy & play the game. The game is even better with friends, try it out. I finally have a dedicated crew to play with and less having to deal with random fills.  I also appreciate the variety of play that can be done in solo's. This game turns into a horror game/stealthmaxxing simulator. TONS of spooky solo moments are to be had on Night Dire Marsh especially.   Overall: 11/10   [very based] Highly recommend to those who love the thrill of the hunt in FPS games and that rush when you make it out of it alive.

My wife’s boyfriend says this is a game for people like me that were excited for NFT’s.  It was Bungie's last chance before Sony took it behind a barn to put it out of its misery… and I think they did a pretty good job.  Yeah, I know Marathon at first looks like a game made by corporate shareholders, but tbh it’s the first game that’s made me feel addicted since Elden Ring. It’s literally Spies vs Mercs if you play Solo or Rook, which feels incredible. I haven’t had this much fun with a multiplayer in years. Not since Splinter Cell: Double Agent. Personally what I liked about Destiny was the intense moments, whether that was in the crucible or in the final boss fight of a raid. Marathon is the intense moments from beginning to end. Losing has consequences and winning has consequences and that's what I love about it. It's not for the faint of heart that's for sure but if you like action, there's no better game.  Yeah the game at first looks like an epileptic's nightmare during a mushroom trip but Marathon has an incredibly strong atmosphere and lore that builds up on the original trilogy and great gunplay to booth. It doesn't completely feel like Destiny, but it also never tries to. Think of it more like a slow burn horror game if you solo, or a cool tactical game with lots of mysteries to unravel. What makes Marathon unique for me is that combination of the gunplay and the commitment to the games premise, atmosphere, and lore. It does a phenomenal job of making you feel like you’re on a hostile world trying to figure out wtf happened. It’s hard to describe why it’s so good besides the obvious things like it being a bungie pvp game. It makes me feel like I’m playing Halo again but in a survival pvp framework with an actual personal character you level up and watch grow. This game should be like crack to any group of friends with a sense of adventure and even a single competitive bone in their body.  The gunplay again captures something magical that I haven’t really felt since the Halo 3 days. It feels nostalgic, and it’s amplified by the fact that you are literally running through the very human remains of a settlement on a distant planet. On top of all of that it exists in a relatively new genre trying some really interesting new things. Bungie knows how to create beautifully rich and interesting worlds and it shows here. So, did you like the lore of quests in D2? Did you enjoy the triumph lists? Do you enjoy Bungie's flavor of gunplay? Have you wanted to feel to a Guardian as a Marine was to a Spartan? Guess what, Marathon's got ya.   Marathons systems are fulfilling. They work together to make your brain fill satisfied as you gain game knowledge. The best games ever do this. That's why it's similar to Destiny my No 1 game of all time. People think they play games for 'fun' but ultimately it's about fulfilment...and Marathon is a masterpiece at that. You are pulled through the screen and every movement and nuances is perfect. Everytime you pull a gun there is no gank it's all you 100% with no crap controls or BS. Game is as tight as you can possibly get and then you have emergent gameplay. Every game plays out different every crew fill a different dynamic. Only negativities are after many hours it can get very samy as sweats queue against each other...they need to sort that out to keep it fresh.  But seriously, what makes Marathon fun? What made Destiny fun? Did you play contest mode raids blind? Did you solo grandmaster nightfalls using a weird Mechaneer’s Tricksleeves build that made you invincible somehow? Were you ever late for work because you stayed up all night spamming a four feat dungeon boss for hours to get the only lightweight frame sword with eager edge? Did you spam control matches trying different ways to proc Lethal Current on a quest to find the weirdest way to oneshot people? Did you play and enjoy Trials of Osiris? Did you learn how to shatter skate? Did you ever run a build that needs triple heavy handed mods? Did you tune in every week to find out if Nimbus could use The Veil to save Osiris from the Radial Mast?  If the answer to any three of those questions is yes, then we've stepped into a war with the Cabal on Mars. I don’t have time to explain what I don’t have time to explain. Marathon is the best shooter if you like shooters. If you ever enjoyed camping, or sniping, or flanking, or rushing, you would already be playing. It’s the best one if you’re a sicko. Not if you’re a scrub. Ignore the hype, drop the market analysis, it’s the next step of their innovation, whether we wanted or not. It’s Bungie at their peak.  Why? Are you some sort of scrub? Are you a moaner? Entitled much? Are you troubled by ‘sweats’? Often worried about ‘try hards’. Sore loser? Are you one of those? Can’t take it huh, huh? HUH? If the answer to any one of those is yes, run away and play Expeditions: A Mudrunner game. Why don’t you run away and play Divinity Original Sin 2: Enchanced edition like lil cute baby? Eyes up Guardian. Go away with your little pals and play Harvest Moon DS - The Tale of Two Towns. Get the hell out of here and shut up. Shooting things is satisfying, it looks visually stunning, and it has dope lore. Only way you’ll truly know if you like it is to try it for yourself, so jump in to see if it’s your jam. Hop on, it’s good.  9/10

I played Destiny 1 and Destiny 2 for over 2,000 hours each (probably more if you combine my Steam and console time). At first, I was very reluctant to play Marathon because most of what I saw came from Destiny players talking about it, and the majority of those opinions were pretty negative. Because of that, I wrote the game off and ignored anything related to it.  Fast forward to Bungie’s free week, and I decided to download it and give it a shot after seeing random people on TikTok saying it was good. I also checked the Steam reviews at the time, which were listed as “Very Positive.” I was immediately struck by the familiarity of the items, vendors, and quest systems. Having played Tarkov for over 5,000 hours, a lot of it felt familiar, but at the same time Marathon had a different vibe that I couldn’t quite place.  At first, I thought, “This game is okay,” but after three or four days, I was absolutely hooked. From the art style, to the way the weapons and attachments work, to the different team synergies you can create with various shells, everything just clicked for me. After that, I purchased the game and the battle pass, and I’ve been loving it ever since. I feel like if people would actually try the game and give it a true chance they may be surprised at what they experience. Marathon 2026 is the game I’ve been waiting decades for.  The gameplay is buttery smooth. The gunplay feels incredible. The lore and storytelling are top-notch and trust the player to piece things together rather than spoon-feeding every detail. Every run leaves me wanting to know more about the world.  What really makes Marathon special, though, is its cultural DNA.  There are influences from everywhere: anime and manga, cyberpunk novels like Neuromancer, games like Wipeout, Jet Set Radio, Rez, and Bust A Move, and shows like Super Milk Chan and Ghost in the Shell. You can feel the influence of early-2000s graphic design houses like The Designers Republic, Attik, Tomato, Me Company, and the work of Hideyuki Tanaka. Their vision of the future wasn’t sleek corporate minimalism—it was loud, experimental, information-dense, colorful, and weird.  That aesthetic shaped how I imagined the future as a teenager. I remember making terrible graphics on a pirated copy of Illustrator, covering everything with kanji characters and Designers Republic-inspired typography because that felt like what the future was supposed to look like. For years there were countless cyberpunk games that tried to capture that feeling, but most never quite got there. They looked cyberpunk, but they didn’t feel futuristic.  Marathon does.  It’s one of the first games I’ve played that feels genuinely otherworldly. Not just because of the technology in the world, but because of the visual language, the storytelling, the typography, the music, the interfaces, and the atmosphere. It’s a future built from decades of influences across graphic design, anime, literature, music, and games.  This art direction is exactly what I’ve always wanted from a video game. Marathon isn’t just inspired by those influences—it synthesizes them into something that feels new.  A masterpiece.  Edit: I have over 300 hours on PS5 and play exclusively on that platform, but I’m leaving reviews on every platform I bought the game (I bought it on every platform). I originally tried Marathon because it was available for free, but I actually enjoyed it enough to buy the game afterward. The core gameplay feels interesting and there is definitely potential here.  However, the UI feels very overwhelming and even a bit claustrophobic at times. For a new player, many things are not easy to understand, and the game does not explain itself very well. It takes more effort than it should to figure out what is going on.  The biggest problem for me is that the game feels too narrow, dark, and visually heavy. It can become tiring and uncomfortable very quickly. Around 50 of my friends started playing the game, but after only 3 matches, many of them said it felt too confusing, too cramped, and too suffocating to continue. The idea behind the game is good, but in its current state, it does not feel welcoming enough.  Another issue is that many players are not playing the game because most of the player base can be quite toxic. This creates a negative atmosphere and makes it harder for new players to enjoy and stick with the game.  Also, the lack of Turkish language support is really disappointing. Many games in this genre come with Turkish localization nowadays, so it feels strange and honestly unnecessary that Marathon does not include it.  Overall, I like the game and I do not regret buying it, but the UI needs improvement, the visual design should feel more open and comfortable, the community atmosphere should be better, and Turkish language support should definitely be added. The concept is strong, but the game needs to feel less dark, less cramped, and easier to understand for new players.

The friend that nagged me to play this game, hates extraction shooters. I, hate extraction shooters. The friend I nagged to play this game, hates extraction shooters. And the friend they nagged to play, also hates extraction shooters.   Yet all of us are absolutely and completely hooked. If you have doubts because you hate extraction shooters, give this game a try anyways. The gameplay, the gunplay, the mechanics, its all just so incredibly well done. The gunplay specifically really takes you back to the Halo multiplayer days - and is just so incredibly enjoyable. Even losing fights is fun. Everything just feels good.  Unlike most other extraction games, gear fear is at a minimum, because the game really does just throw gear at you. The lower 80-90% of gear is not that difficult to obtain - and even someone using the lower 5% of gear has a legitimate opportunity to take on a fully kitted team in that top 10%.   The gear in this game certainly makes an impact, and its enough to be noticeable and desirable. But everything is so well balanced, in my opinion, that you are never in a scenario where you feel overwhelmingly behind or uncompetitive.  The lore is rich and deep and it is one of very few games where I am deeply interested in the story, even if it takes a back seat to the overall gameplay. As someone who has never enjoyed a single extraction shooter, I truly cannot recommend this game enough.

I can't really compare this to other E-Shooters but as far as I can tell Marathon is a fair bit more "arcadey" than the others; games are fairly quick and the loot, free gear and sponsored loadouts are so abundant that the only time you ever feel like youre starting with absolutely nothing is at the start of a new season with the inventory wipe.  If you like Destiny or Borderlands, this game is like those on steroids. If you like arena shooters, weirdly enough, I think this game will scratch a similar itch. It certainly does for me; if I find myself at a disadvantage in a fight I KNOW that it is because I did something wrong. The gear you bring in adds some real felt stakes to the fights, but you get over the ennui of losing your crap immediately after kitting out for the next match. Its definitely not as mindlessly easy as Halo, Apex, CoD or Destiny, but the fights and rewards feel oh so worth the difficulty. And there are actually a lot of cosmetics to unlock in gameplay! My crew and I came in to Marathon interested in what Bungie did, but were here to deride and laugh at it. What we instead found was a wildly intelligent game with incredible design. The gameplay is buttery smooth, looting easy and direct. The guns play phenomenally. The story is tons of fun!  The mini and mega dungeons are a real standout feature I enjoy SO much.  You are always overloaded with gear. The shells are SO fun, all of them.  General Gameplay: You spend most of your time making a large amount of micro-decisions. Where am I going, what am I here for, what is my "win" condition, etc. You also need to weigh up if making noise is worth it, since going loud of course means you are going to attract attention, but you also will be moving faster and getting more loot by breaking bots.  Like other games of this genre, it takes a short amount of time to realize what is and isn't worth looting or what to prioritize. The economy is mostly focused around trade-in items and cash. They can often be used interchangeably for low to medium tier items, so you are never without essentials even early on. Things like ammunition can be traded for with easy to find and stack items.  Shells aka Classes: All of the Shells in the game are quite powerful and intelligently designed. You get two to three actives and one to two passives. They all work well on their own but some shells really compliment each other. A shell like Triage can obviously go it alone as a solo medic, but his revive can also be used offensively to create space for a Shell like destroyer to blast it.  Gunplay: Guns feel almost exactly like Halo, and some guns literally ARE halo guns. It's a really REALLY standout feature how good it feels to move and shoot.  [ESCAPE WILL MAKE ME HORSE]

Marathon is my personal game of the year. That's actually quite unexpected, since I was never a big competitive PvP player and I bounced off Arc Raiders for various reasons. I didn't even have Marathon on my radar and only reluctantly agreed to try it out during the server slam with a friend.  First, I have a very strong emotional attachment to the game's aesthetics and overall vibe. I love the art, the lore and the music. I even ended up buying the collector's edition after I had already purchased and played the game. I'm the kind of player who will never like a live service game if I don't like its style.  The PvP is the core game play loop and it's extremely well done. It's definitely on the sweaty side and I had to record my runs to learn from my mistakes. But the feeling of winning a 1v3 and turning the whole run around and extracting with great loot is unmatched. More importantly though, the PvP is just really well done. The runner shells (= classes) and utilities (= grenades, etc.) add some depth to the encounters, levels are well made and reward good positioning, and most guns have great feedback and therefore feel fun to shoot. It goes so far that I sometimes just join a run with a free kit and hunt other players, not really caring about progression or loot. The core loop of run, jump and shoot is already very enjoyable.  Personally, I also really like what they've done with faction progression and the armory. By completing various contracts (= quests) you level up factions. This gives you better access at purchasable items, which also includes daily free items. Taken together, all this slowly but steadily elevates your "loot floor", meaning you have more and more, easier access to decent load outs. This helps with gear fear.  It's pretty rare for an extraction shooter to also have a ranked mode. It probably still needs a few iterations, but it's already a pretty cool idea for those looking for really intense PvP. Cheating is a bit of an issue though (based on streamers and hearsay).  One warning: you can't play the entire game using only solo queue. It's a big point of contention at the moment. The endgame map Cryo Archive is only available to trios. Now personally I put in some effort over 2 weeks to make some in game friends and now I have enough people I vibe with so that I can usually do my runs with a premade trio.  If you don't feel comfortable doing that, then relying on solo + crew fill (= playing with randoms) could really sour your experience.  ----  TL;DR: My main and only game this year.

I started off being extremely cautious about this game from the very beginning. At the very earliest, I wrote the game off because I was not a fan of Bungie after I put D2 down years back. When the alleged art theft came out, I actively disliked the game's development. After that was resolved, playing the game in the closed alphas put me off again; thinking that the game felt clunky, generic, and empty. About the only thing I really liked about the game was the artstyle, but that was far from enough to get me to like the game  Then came the Server Slam, and I gave it a final try, and it hooked me easier than any game I've played before. Preordered it right before release, and I've been addicted since. The BungieTM gunplay, the palpable atmosphere, its one of a kind artstyle, the captivating worldbuilding (though I wish was more front and center), and the tense, heart-pumping fights that always leave me motivated to go back and get even better... I'll never say this game is perfect, but god does it have so much of all I could want from an extraction shooter, and it earned my love for it through hard-fought turnaround.  And note, I say all of this having experienced all of Season 1 while working 12 hours a day 5 days a week, and I still had time to boot this game up, have fun, and even get to Compiler.  I only hope more people can see past the irrational, baseless hate and assumptions -that this game is supposed to be for everyone- for long enough to try it, and find out that they might really love it the way I came to adore it.

As a long-term Destiny player, I think it's important to highlight that this really is an amazing game with so much potential going into the future. I admit that this game isn't for everyone, and it hurts to see Destiny end, but I don't blame Marathon for that failure, and really hope Marathon continues long into the future.     Marathon is difficult, with a steep learning curve, and early progress feels like everything is tuned so tightly that you can barely make it through a run, but as time goes on, that same tight tuning becomes its greatest strength once you learn enough. I think recent additions like Night Marsh and especially Sponsored Night Marsh are great additions that make the game feel scary and deeply immersive, but also easier, and allow players to learn events and map interactions without being ganked by a stacked team. I would argue that TTK is too low, especially with early shields, and that a LOT can be done to improve onboarding new players, but what is here is truly worth it.     With PVE additions and highest tier gear locked to standard modes I think this game can appeal to a bigger audience, and I want it to have a lifespan long enough to see it get there. The vibes are truly amazing and the immersion in the world, gunplay, shell kits and tense engagements make it an easy recommend, but new modes and onboarding are needed to accomodate more casual audiences, myself included.    Oh and I have been playing via Boosteroid on a macbook Air and miraculously there is almost no lag or latency issues, which is wild

Hmmm where to begin. Marathon on paper is exactly everything I could ever want. The aesthetic, transhumanism story and mysterious setting have limitless potential that is unfortunately limited by its primary game mode. Don't get me wrong, I enjoy the extraction aspect of this game, but it almost feels like it holds it back sometimes.  Extractions shooters have the problem of Quests feeling very Samey over time. Most of them tend to fall into fetch quests which can be tolerable at the beginning but become a chore as theyre the only way to progress the story and their "single run" requirement can be frustrating if youre playing with people who just don't really care.   At times I feel like im not exactly sure what to loot, and most of the time im not really looting anything at all unless its from another runner. I come from Arc Raiders where every loot can either be used in crafting or recycling which added value. Since we can't really craft weapons, Im at sort of a loss of what I should be on the look out for with looting.   All in all I find myself itching to play more. Even if I might not get fully sucked in I keep coming back for a few runs here and there. So while on sale at 27.99 its probably worth the price, but 40 is asking for a lot.   So for now, if youre itching for a new FPS experience that blends extraction shooter with cyber dystopian horror then this might be worth the buy. I also hear that a full PVE mode is in the works that might be worth it? It will depend on how that mode contributes towards progression ecosystem.   really wish they added a campaign because a fully fledged story of an awakening runner remembering who they were would be sooo good. but alas. thanks sony

Coming from a casual that is not very good at the game, Marathon is just stunning and the improvements for season 2 are just incredible.     I played a bunch of season 1 and I had tons of fun and fell in love with the game. The insane art design, addictive gunplay mechanics, the lore, absolutely stunning audio design, it's just remarkable and it has perhaps some of the best art direction of a game, ever. But ultimately, I felt mixed as I progressed more and I started getting paired with hardcore players. It completely halted my progress and took the fun out of the game.     But for this season, the inclusion of PvE content solved most of my issues with the game. It's nice to decompress, do some quests and find new loot on the new PvE mode, and more importantly, I feel more relaxed when I die in PvP because I just know that I can cue up for PvE and get something cool again.     Not only that, when I cue up for PvP I go there actually craving the PvP since I satisfied other aspects of my gameplay experience through the PvE mode. It feel like a win-win.     I just wished that they had all maps available for PvE, or at least a rotating playlist because it feels like a big ask, if you are cueing with randoms, to go around the map to complete the objectives for the main quests. I really want to do them, but most randoms don't want to. I would rather do those main quest during PvE in solo fill and then use all of that gear in PvP trio fill.     Highly recommend to jump in during season 2 if you have been on the fence!

As someone who’s played many FPS games (including several extraction and BR types) over the years, I can now confidently give my review on this game based on the initial time spent playing it:  + Great character diversity (even if only very small amount in roster currently), with cool voice lines and commands that you can adapt to easily + Maps feel very unique in strange and bizarre ways, especially with the IKEA-esque polygonal laboratory aesthetic and weird structures all over that make it feel like a utopian and dystopian cyberpunk reality both ways + Quests and challenges are similar to ARC Raiders or Hunt Showdown, and feel pretty easy and intuitive for the most part (not sure if this holds true at later level though) + Customizations (skins, weapon camps, etc) are available to grind for or even buy with real money, making it easy for players to feel incentivized to play more often + Most players you group up through matchmaking seem to be social and friendly/helpful (based on my personal experiences queuing in with randoms many times) + Almost every exfiltration point is RNG based per match, making it more fun and entertaining to find your way out or escape from the match (no fixed spawn points add a layer of complexity in a challenging way) + The atmospheric audio and loading screen glitchiness stuff is really neat and visually appealing  - Playerbase and mixed reviews based on Bungie’s poor decision making forced this game to become a sloppy mess over time (financially, logistically, and other reasons) - The game has not so many characters yet due to slow builds and development over time, leading to faster disengagement from players - The mundane polygonal atmospheres of maps may steer people away from the game due to how odd and confusing some environments are, even more so at night time on certain maps - Most areas feel like they were designed with “glass half empty” perspective, making it feel like the game is missing some sense of accomplishment - Game lacks a bit of optimization, contrary to other gamer perspectives (I usually lag in random places on certain maps, so many instances are actually based on how good your rig is) - There are sweaty enemy teams (besides the usual AI bots) that love to camp and annoy new players and veterans alike, so be prepared for griefing teams all the time - Many challenges and goals seem repetitive, not to mention doing the same exfiltration process on every map - Bungie did not really seem to learn from its past mistakes of implementing microtransactions, as this game continues the same trend (understandable as it is a product that feeds employees and puts food on the table for their families, but money seems to always be a major factor in making these games)  Overall, I recommend this game to anyone who likes extraction shooters and fun co-op sessions. The cyberpunky vibes will definitely reel you in, if nothing else!

Here's the thing about Marathon.... it is the best extraction shooter on the market. The problem is, that means you have to enjoy extraction shooters. PvP especially. It's not a hug box like Arc Raiders. players WILL shoot you, and they WILL try to do so from advantageous situations (ratting, ambushes, 3rd party, etc). Such is the nature of the beast with extraction shooters and PvP.    If you don't like PvP or you think you need to win EVERY run, you will not like Marathon. I'm going to compare it to Arc Raiders as that is the most recent, massively "successful" extract shooter:    Marathon has better gunplay.  Marathon has better PvE.  Marathon has more interesting environments (though this is debatable if you don't like the aesthetic)  Marathon is not as casual friendly.  Marathon is still very much experimenting with "what works" (as of June 2026) so some features will be changing, sometimes by a lot.  Marathon is first person, which for me is 2000% better in any competitive FPS. 3rd person is fine if you like 3-peeking and infinite information, but to me Arc Raiders characters are boring to look at, i don't enjoy that style of combat and Marathon is just so much more immersive.  Marathon is made by Bungie, for better or worse it is a Bungie game through and through.... do with that what you will

I'm 12 years old again playing Minecraft Hunger Games. I've got a bunch of gear equipped that's pretty good but if I run into the wrong person its wraps. I crouch to stay hidden as I loot another box until I hear faint footsteps creeping behind. But when I turn around, there's nothing to be seen. I change hiding spots, scanning the area for anything that moves. Others mean danger. Some might be friendly, but that's not really how things go around here.    Marathon differs from classic HG in that the objective is only to extract at the designated points and not necessarily to be the last player standing. This means if you do find the rare friendlies in a lobby, you can decide to work together to survive and successfully win together. You can then send a friend request and load up in a team with them if you feel like it. The message is clear: the world is brutal and draining, but the connections you manage to make and the small moments of humanity will always shine through, even if they aren't always rewarded. After all, the greed and violence that is the norm is just as human.

TL:DR - Free Trial played for hours, didn't jive. Thought about it and wanted to free trial a bit more to give it a chance, started to jive more. Really good game, but rough to get into.  Free trial play for a handful of hours and overall, the game is gorgeous to look at, but it's very cumbersome/cluttered beyond enjoyment...  I cannot say this game is "unenjoyable" due to my limited time and so many upgrades/level-ups not being obtained yet, but the initial first impression was not fun. Movement is very sluggish, objects/enemies blend into the environment, UI/Lobby is over the top with pop-ups and messages, half the time I feel like I'm having a Cyberpunk Stroke. Maybe I've become too old for this genre.  Re-Review - So, after the initial 5-6hrs free trial play, I had some lingering thoughts and interest in the games world overall that drove me to continue playing further with the free "weekend". Without spiraling into paragraphs of description, I'll try to highlight real quick what changed.  I've been playing a lot of action shooter titles, D2, Fortnite, RE, Overwatch, Remnant 2, Dead Space, etc. and this game was throwing me for a loop due to the fact that I had to completely change my gameplay style. My initial frustration with going into a run and being obliterated was very much due to the fact that I was just trying to Rambo things. You CANNOT do that in this game, at least not when first starting out. Once I started to change that and be cautious about my every action, this game became MUCH more enjoyable.  The sound quality in this is HUGE. Playing with surround sound speakers or headset really make a difference. When combat can erase you in mere seconds, E V E R Y moment you can spare matters. You begin to learn sounds and understand what is an NPC/Bot sound vs another runner.  I could go into much deeper detail about all different aspects, but I just want to say one more thing. This game is beautiful, fun, and lures you in with some interesting vibes, but it is ROUGH. It's like a song or movie you watch the first time and think "This is kinda weird and dumb...", but it sticks in your head, so you try it again and begin to like it. It's definitely not a game for everyone. It's a game that you need to be in the right mindset for. I'll be playing it more and bettering my old gamer brain.

I was on the fence about this game. I have played other extraction shooters like Tarkov and Delta Force and they didn't vibe with me. The only similar game I ever liked was DMZ in MW2 which was killed after a year. I even debated refunding this game all the way up to the 2 hour mark. Initially I didn't like the art style, I was like "what is this Battlefield 2042 color scheme?" but honestly its grown on me a lot. Its like if future computers and technology kept the old DOS aesthetic. I debated between this and Arc Raiders and what sold me was the aim assist being better since I mainly play on controller and that there is no SBMM. I have also been a long time Halo/Bungie fan and felt  needed to at least give it a chance. I've mainly been playing solo but have done Trios a few times here and there and its a fun experience just doing the quests. Dying doesn't feel awful like it does in other evac's, just awe I died, oh well. I'm having a lot of fun just doing missions for the factions and even getting tidbits about the lore through them. As I learn and get better I enjoy the game more and more. I even bought the Deluxe edition upgrade.     As for the negatives, performance isn't great. Even on a Ryzen 9/RTX 4090 Laptop I'm getting 93-113 FPS on average with DLSS Quality at 1440p at mid to low settings. I see people using Framegen which is abysmal for this type of PVE/PVP focused game. Honestly as long as you get over 60 FPS you should be fine. I've also heard the servers haven't been great but I have yet to experience any issues.

This game is VERY good.  I've never played an extraction shooter before as previous entries always seemed either a little too jank, or a little too kumbaya. I was waiting for one that looked slick.  Good grief Marathon is exactly what I wanted. This game is a tense adventure in a strange and intriguing future punctuated by huge plays and devastating misplays. You can play like a superhero or a bastard. It's magnificent.  This is not a game for everyone. There are a lot of mistakes a person can make, especially regarding making noise, and it isn't always immediately obvious what consequence followed what mistake. This can be frustrating. Solo missions are very good at making it obvious what will get you killed, chiefly making useless noise, being exposed, not using all your tools, and not paying attention to your own corners and audio.   After some time in solo (which was incredibly educational), queueing with 3 man fill has been a great experience. Some teams are dumb, but there are many friendly people on mic to go on these adventures with. Paying attention, being honest with myself about what I could have done better, and watching other successful players, I'm getting better and the good has far outweighed the bad. The game is tense, exciting, atmospheric, and dramatic. The gunplay is phenomenal. Exploring it and spending time in it has been a blast, and I already have crossed the point where it was worth what I paid for it.  Here is my warning: the game massively rewards things like patience, deliberate play, understanding of positioning, and cooldowns in ways that remind me of a MOBA. I think the narrative the game is full of tryhards is downstream of the fact that playing well produces enormous dividends, and if you just wanna pick up loot and shoot robots and not pay attention to everything else going on, you're gonna get absolutely destroyed. That always divides people, and it's difficult if not impossible to make a game that fully caters to both crowds. This game didn't try to, so manage your expectations. You've gotta meet the game where it is at. If you do, and don't attach it to your ego too much either way, it's great. If you feel like your purchase entitles you to immediate gratification and a power fantasy, it's gonna be rough sailing.  If you are okay with failing a bit while you learn, this game might be for you, and if you are the sort of person this game is for, it's REALLY for you, so good grief get in here and try it.  This is an INCREDIBLE game for the people it is for.

I'm 17 hours into the free play day period and my thoughts are:  This is one of the better "Triple A" releases in 2026, or probably in the past couple of years, especially for an extraction shooter and from Bungie. Coming from Cod and other first person shooters, this game does a lot different to begin with. The artstyle and/or visuals are niche but are appealing subjectively. The gunplay does feel similar to the likes of destiny or halo in a similar sense but still manages to be distinct through the use of different rounds (bullet types) in the extraction shooter setting. E.g. volt batterys that shoot "volts" and then bullet projectile weapons using light or heavy rounds.   To address the games features: 1. The game is decently optimized. I own a budget PC and playing games that run "well" are my number one priority. The minimum requirements ask for a 1050 Ti, but i believe a GTX 1650 is probably more realistic, or perhaps a GTX 1060. I use a 1650 super and get 60fps (+ or -) usually almost all of the time on low settings. I won't deny that the game is well optimized, for a 2026 title, it is VERY optimized. No stutters, freezes or major crashes game is on an ssd, 25gb (at time of this review), no low framerates, etc. The only problem i suffer from, is if i shift-tab and use steam overlay/browser, the game crashes. Alt-tabbing is fine, which will probably be what you do in this game. In mentioning Optimization, the graphics are simple but effective. They look quite nice in addition to the aesthetic and artstyle, even though i play at low. I prefer this over an unoptimized unreal engine 5 garbage slop game.   2. The gameplay. At first i thought this game would get repetitive quick. 17 hours might not be much but I have wholeheartedly enjoyed these 17 hours of time played. I've completed several quests and noticed that they are either simple, or advanced to an extent. Quests are repetitive if it's a difficult challenge or just "go here, do x, and leave". Most quests after early game, especially when going into the map "Dire Marsh" go something along the lines of "Go here, spawn this boss/mini boss in, kill it, get this item, go here" and then you're done, or a variation of these objectives. The quests definitely feel unique and distinct, my favourite aspect is the combination of kill quests, where kills are counted for killing "hostiles" rather than just player enemies. This gives the game a PVEVP or PVE + PVP aspect, similar to Tarkov or other extraction shooters. This makes the quest easier, though you are incentivized to kill runners, because they will progress quests quicker. Conclusively, while runs might get repetitive, quests and actual gameplay + PVP does not, every encounter usually feels and plays different even if you run to the same location all the time.   3. Gunplay is quite decent and even satisfactory in this game. As mentioned earlier you have a fair amount of weapons. I haven't encountered shotguns yet, but i have encountered volt weapons, (thrower, punch and railgun + an ar i forget the name of) and have enjoyed them, in addition to the heavy round and light round weapons. Weapons or guns generally satisfactory, gun sounds are nice and feel punchy. Some enemies may feel spongy, in addition to players, this is likely because of the shield addition to the game, in addition to your health. This is fine in my opinion, this makes combat open-ended, giving you the option to run off or stay in the fight, making you weigh up the pros and cons of staying in battle or running off.   4. Addressing what is probably the elephant in the room, the hero's or "Shells". I don't believe the Shells are a bad addition to the game at all, i believe they are all quite balanced unless the people i'm versing don't know how to use them. You'll have a passive ability and 3 main abilities. I have been maining Sentinel and have loved his ability, which consists of a Jager like alt from siege, a Snare or a placeable proximity mine, and a player scanner that operates similiar to a heart scanner or UAV from COD. The only Shell that could be annoying is Assassins ability to cloak, usually you can still hear an Assassin player creeping around, otherwise i don't find myself dying specifically to a Shell's ability, rather just gunfire, third-party, shot from behind or just the classic "skill-issue". I'm not a pro by any means, but i'm not picking up a tryhard meta in this game, it seems quite open ended from being "good at shooters" to "understanding your shell's ability/knowing the map and how to play it correctly". There are certainly good or "meta" weapons, such as one of the SMG's which has a significantly high rate of fire, to some of the DMR's or Sniper weapons. Subjectively, I'm very comfortable playing Sentinel, he works in PVP with spamming his Snare and UAV when in a gunfight, allowing you to hunt down enemies or lock down a location.   5. Since this is an extraction shooter, we should address the economy. It is forgiving if you compare this game to Tarkov. To start with, you have unlimited free kits. Yes, the kit isn't usually that good, it can range from a pistol to an LMG and the gun usually doesn't have any upgrades, you don't have any shields, only an ammo crate, basic heals and ammo, but still. In addition, you can usually get free daily items from your factions if you progress enough. In addition to BOTH of these freebies, there's ROOK which is a scav, similar to Tarkov. Again, in addition to these THREE, early-game and for most quest rewards, you usually are rewarded with weapons and ammo or heals, etc. This game is generous early on, and even when your quests only reward items to upgarde your faction traders, you still can access a free kit every day that is unlimited. In my opinion, gear fear isn't much of a thing in this game, which can appeal to casual players.  Are there any negatives? Honestly, not really. If i had to be critical, the game could have better optimization, by getting rid of the shift+tab steam crash or could produce higher framerates, given the simple graphics in terms of shadows, textures etc. However, all things considered, this is a 2026 triple A title and it could not run at all on my system, or run terribly.  In terms of the gameplay, it is satisfactory and would land for me a 8-8.5/10. It's not realistic like Tarkov, it's more arcadey, it has a light hero shooter element, the economy is forgiving, guns are punchy for the most part, the game is optimized and the devs don't seem greedy, considering the games low price and lack of obvious bundle slop in the store. That being said, most cosmetics are free and are earned through progress, e.g. using a free kit and extracting with it rewards the shell you extracted with the outfit with a style from the faction the kit is from. Or you can get a skin for your knife by kiilling a hostile, not specifically a player, but an NPC enemy or a player, from behind. Which is very easy.   If you're into Halo/Destiny, interested in Marathon/Marathon lore (as this game has previous entries) and extraction shooters, don't want a sweatfest and something that could be played casually, you will definitely enjoy Marathon.  Lastly, there are mentions of a PVE mode and a PVP-lite mode, which would be awesome additions. I believe Bungie is indeed cooking with Marathon.

Hours speak for themselves. Game isn't as intimidating as "extraction shooter" as a label makes it sound. You can do a lot of silly things. If you frequently use prox chat and are chill, you're very likely to find other chill people or even full chill lobbies. (Not every game-- but often.) Plenty of options to do the blood-for-the-blood-god route, as well. (Hell, half the time people you down will still GG you in prox chat. Not that mad people don't exist, just that there's a lot more chill than you might expect)  Assess sub-5-hour, product received for free reviews with a massive grain of salt. Amazing aesthetic, music, and sound design. Incredible world building. This is a challenging game for thoughtful people that love great gameplay and don't need to be spoonfed wins and achievements every time they play.

Onboarding is generally poor. There's textboxes up on textboxes to explain menus which sucks to read. The starter mission is on the worst most basic map. But if you stick with it, and you ignore the Bungie drama and the Destiny hate, you will see where at least some of that $250m and 5 years went.  Visually, it may not be everyone's cup of tea. It is not safe. And if you love grey and beige cardigans, good for you. But if you like art (especially stolen art) and risk there is much to enjoy and appreciate. And it runs great on mid-range PCs.  The sound design is top tier. I can clearly distinguish player sounds. Directionality is spot on, which is so important in a game like this. Whereas ARC Raiders for example, I struggled to understand where sounds were coming from. Even those little Raiders caches, I could not pick out where they were from a few feet away.  The gunplay is industry leading. I played Destiny 1 & 2 for years. It was the gunplay and story that kept me engaged for thousands of hours. I'm so happy to play a Bungie game again and find weapons that hit right and feel so good to shoot.  The progression system has been largely overhauled in season 2. As a solo player in season 1 it was a long grind I was not going to push through, especially as I started late. But season 2 and the introduction of the cradle is a better system and it has been sped up considerably to the point where I am confident I will reach level 100 this season.   The new night mode Dire Marsh is fun and they should for sure do something similar to Perimeter to breathe life into the beginner map.   Now, is this a commercial success for Bungie and Sony? No. I shouldn't care, but it does place that doubt in your mind that the game you are enjoying will not be around for a long time. But while it is here and they're continuing to support the game by adding more casual modes and   Overall, I love the gameplay. And I love the fact that Bungie made Marathon. Thank you.  Now I just need to find some friends who don't enjoy beige to play with. ;)

I honestly didn't know what to expect from Marathon, but that turned out to be a great thing. It's a completely new experience, IMO. The market is flooded with extraction shooters right now, so it’s actually refreshing to see a game with its own unique aesthetic and mechanics (don't worry, the core extraction elements are still there). And yes, I've played most of them: Tarkov, Arc Raiders, Arena Breakout Infinite, Hunt: Showdown, The Forever Winter, Delta Force, Marauders, Sulfur... you name it.  Look, I get why people are mad at Bungie, but projecting that hate onto the actual gameplay makes zero sense. The game is doing its own thing, and that's allowed. Imagine if we all just agreed to blindly play one single game per genre for the rest of time. The gaming world is huge—step outside your bubble and actually try something different.

I have never been a fan of extraction shooters but as I grew up on Halo and Destiny, I still wanted to check this game out just to try it. Luckily I was given the chance to try the game out from Bungie, but I never expected to really like it. I thought I'd play it for a bit then just go back to playing Destiny 2... #WeWantDestiny3     The game is quite hardcore and unforgiving at times (you die a lot) but I very quickly noticed that every time I died the circumstances surrounding the death became so engrained into my brain that I would often be thinking about each scenario long after I had logged off. When coming across certain areas of each map, I will vividly remember my previous deaths in that area with great detail (exactly how they happened, what I was doing at those times and what I should do better this time). Of course this happens in most games to a degree, but something about the high stakes of Marathon makes these moments particularly memorable. This also makes the big wins incredibly rewarding and can make multiple failed runs feel worth it.     Aside from all of that... The game is just fun. The gunplay is the classic Bungie FPS feel, so gunfights feel great. Servers are really stable (from my experience). Prox chat is hilarious and through this I have met a lot of really cool fun people since launch. I am excited to see how the game progresses with the story and hope more people will ignore the silly online hate and just try the game out for themselves.     The game is definitely not for everyone... but it may surprise you (like it did to me).

I will presage this by saying I originally intended to give this game a WIDE swerve. I played D2 for a few years and Bungies behaviour with that title meant I was absolutely loathe to give them my time and money again. However, I gave the game a spin during the free week and I am pretty glad I did.     Firstly, this is a beautiful and to me at least a very unique looking game. I have not seen an art style like this before, and my brain keeps using '3D-Printerpunk' as a way of describing it. Vibrant, stimulating and ever so slightly uncanny.     I don't think I need to talk about the gunplay, it is a Bungie title and for all their faults this is their strong point. Near flawless in this regard. I have seen people talk about the UI and menus being difficult to parse and navigate; I am a veteran 4X player, to me these elements of the game are fine. YMMV.     For me the big draw here is the atmosphere the game manages to generate and in part this is down to some phenomenal sound design. the maps *sound* alive, and for me the big skill I am having to develop is to discern what is background noise and what is someone coming to delete me. tense, spooky, and paranoia inducing. Exactly what one wants from this genre.     I am still wary about the potential for monetisation nonsense. This *is* Bungie, after all and I remember the days of Tess in the D2 tower. Thankfully, this seems limited to cosmetics at the moment so it is (at least for me) easy to ignore.     Overall if you are into FPS and have a rig that can handle it, I think you will get your money's worth from this game, especially if it is on sale. It is beautiful, tense and chaotic.

Genuinely a very competent game with very few bugs/issues and very engaging PVE. My biggest problem is that it costs 40$ by default. I remember a time when live-service games ALWAYS released F2P to draw as many people in to buy microtransactions as possible, this is something that Bungie ignored, opting for a borderline full price live service game with battle passes and skins. This is obviously a terrible idea, another terrible idea is the Visual design of the game in relation to skins - i personally find it cool and dont mind it, i just have no clue how youre going to sell skins in a game like this where everything looks like a weird legendary skin by default. Other than that i find the game very good, it reminds me of a great game called The Cycle Frontier, unfortunately that game is dead and you can no longer play it, which puts paying 40$ into further question, considering nobody knows how long this game will live. The game also gives you alot of handouts so youll never run out of gear to run with, in case you do you can queue rook protocol which i found to be the best part of this game and the most efficient way to play. Overall its a good game that i can easily recommend if you like the genre, im just not sure about paying for it, i think it will have a small dedicated playerbase but i have no idea if it will survive.

As an OG Destiny fan, I can confidently say the Bungie magic is fully alive here. The gunplay is absolutely spectacular—in some cases, these weapons feel as crisp, responsive, and satisfying as the best classic god rolls from Destiny or even the golden era of Halo.     Visually, the game oozes style. The art direction is completely out of this world. It’s bold, it's distinct, and while I know that specific aesthetic might not be for everyone, it absolutely hits the mark for me.     Fair warning: it’s an extraction shooter, so you have to be ready for the brutal loop. The highs of escaping with incredible loot and the devastating lows of losing everything on the next run can be polarizing. But man, when those highs hit? They are so incredibly high that I can’t put the game down.     If you have ever enjoyed a Bungie shooter, this is 100% worth picking up. Highly recommended.

Extraction games are hard and will always have a steep learning curve. You WILL run into sweat lords who are 50 levels above you. You WILL die and lose your nice hard earned loot. I haven't played any extraction games where this doesn't happen. Luckily with this game there's a lot of free gear options, even more-so if you level up the factions which again isn't too hard to do as your contracts with them complete even on death. Poor Marathon can't seem to catch a break. First it couldn't escape being compared to Arc Raiders, then the Destiny 2 news broke (a game that was nearly a decade old mind you) and everyone decided it was somehow this title's fault. It's too bad, because if you give it a chance, there a really fun and promising experience to be had here so long as you know what to expect from an extraction shooter, which evidently, a sizeable subset of reviewers do not.  I get the feeling that the bigger issue is a fundamental lack of understanding about what type of experience Marathon is.  It ought to come as no surprise that Bungie's gunplay is great here, and combined with a kick-ass story and a lot of great atmosphere, the world pulls you in bigtime. Like a lot of other people, I didn't give this game much thought on release, but I played during free weekend and that was all it took to convince me to continue. It's a refreshing change of pace from Arc raiders, but not as hardcore as a game like Tarkov; a near-perfect middle-ground that makes me wish I'd paid more attention earlier in time.  It also feels like there's no shortage of things to do in Marathon, unlike some of it's contemporaries. I'm not in the endgame yet but I feel like the game rewards casual play well, and there's a competitive matchmaking bracket apparently which I guess I could see myself checking out late-game.  The only real gripe I have is how opaque menus can be at times, at least as a new player, but it's nothing that you won't become accustomed to after a few runs. All that is to say, don't trust reviews that call this game a 'rouge-lite shooter' or complain because the map spawns you to far from your quest objectives.  There is a lot of fun to be had here for those who actually know what an extraction shooter is.

I do however believe it's okay to love a game on principle and I LOVE Marathon. Nobody with any amount of self-esteem will take that kind of gaslighting and abuse from a corporation you give money to. We all know the future of Marathon, nobody trusts Bungie, and those that do - deserve whats coming.   Sony- do the right thing. Shut them down. Scrap every part of it and sell every asset bargain bin. I am filled with immense joy, but my vengeance for bungie is insatiable.   P.S. Sorry to the employees who tried to make something great just to be sabotaged by corpo over and over.

This Review is unbiased and does not reflect on Bungie/Sony's decisions to continue support for this game while Destiny receives its final content update. User plans to put more time into this game after Review.  Bungie's revival of this 3 decade old IP as a live service extraction shooter is certainly a choice gamble to make considering today's turbulent game industry, let alone the genre. Other competitors like Escape from Tarkov and ARC Raiders already have an established fanbase within the genre and even more time under the developers foundry, not to mention systems and QoL features that players had been asking for, for years.   With that being said, this game is great and I think, with enough time and feedback, could stand up to be a real kicker for the genre.  Let us start with Bungie's most adept skill, Gunplay, Abilities, and Modification. They come out of the gate swinging with weapons that are very reminiscent of Halo both in looks and function, but still feel unique to use and run with during the matches. ARs for those who prefer an All-In-One-Tool, MGs lay down suppressive yet brutal hails of lead, Snipers and Precision for those who like peace and quiet from on high, and Shotguns/SMGs for brutes who like seeing white in a Runners eyes. all feel fulfilling, right for each job given your position on the battlefield is optimal. Abilities bring a bit of spice to the mix in that alongside typical grenades and ammo bags, they are unique to the Runner you choose. Most are intriguing, even Rook who functions as a Scav, spawning late and can blend in with the enemy bots on the map.  On that heel, the abilities each runner can use can be expanded upon in the form of Cores and Implants. One i had specifically found was for the Assassin, allowing for a longer duration to his invisibility skill. very interesting and a good way to add depth to the runner you choose to main. I'm interested to see what builds there are further in. One thing I would like to see is a Shooting range of some kind to test what works and if you want to even try it in practice.  With all that being said, how do these systems fare in a...Target Rich Environment? Let us talk about the Combat.  This is another branch that remains relatively unchanged ever since the debut of Halo: Combat Evolved in 2001, and it remains true that you don't fix what is not broken. The maps I have played so far feel distant enough to be eerie and wary of other players, yet full enough to be a shooting gallery. Outdoor engagements are hectic and messy, leading to 3rd parties thirsty for shell-blood, while indoors its a shoebox of noise and clockwork, while both teams knife so frantically they wonder if a telephone booth is a better option for the fight, up until they kill one another. And with the Time-to-kill in this game it doesn't take long to find the end of a fight, so long as you have enough ammo to wear down shields And reduce them to a bag on the ground. The Bots littered around the map do not make things easier, as they hit just as hard and are stronger in numbers, adding to the difficulty. If you hope to make it to an exfil with something to show for it, you'd better put a bullet in that UESC Commander's head and sprint like its your last day on Tau Ceti IV.  Sprinting isn't just for fleeing however, its how you move forward. Time for Movement. Once again a very similar note being played, however instead of gliding through the air like a bird, its more like prowling in the grass like a lion, No matter who you choose, all have a stamina bar in the form of an Overheat Meter that you need to keep track of, otherwise you are forced to walk until you cool down. implants and mods can change how much or how little thermal cooling you have, which alters how long you can run, similar to the Weight system from Tarkov but putting a unique twist on how to explain it within the world.  it is also how you explain what you're doing here in the first place. Enter Lore and Worldbuilding. However your preference for sci-fi mercenary stories go, it is...mostly well put together. The characters have voices, Contracts explain what you need to do and why, but never truly take you further unless you decide to dig into the Codex, a weak point for Bungie ever since Grimoire cards in Destiny 1. a Tab for specifically lore would be nice, or I have not found it yet.  The maps and walls of text you explore leave more questions for you to ask then answer them, which as far as your characters involvement goes and how you immerse yourself in the story, emboldens your curiosity. Most who build themselves up around lore and stories will uncover the truth about what really happened here. Others are only here to pick fights and follow orders given by heads on a CPU. Neither are bad routes to take, as even as a Bungie game with a story to tell, it is still an Extraction shooter. (PERSONAL NOTE: I think that its genuinely an interesting theme, how you function as a soul in a plastic shell of wires and circuits searching for a reason as to how and why an entire colony ship could just suddenly go AWOL. very cool stuff. more please)  Other notes include: + very Solid Performance for how beautiful it is + Proximity chat for memorable and funny interactions with Runners + Controller Friendly for couch sessions + Atmosphere is thrilling, Dire Marsh at night feels like a special op mission - Vibrancy is nice, but very intensive - Confusing UI for those uninitiated with Extraction shooters or similar - enough rain and water to flood the earth again, desert/winter map across T-C-IV maybe?  And it does all beg the questions, why an Extraction shooter, and does bungie have enough gusto to break into and be a major competitor in the genre? It is a question that I'm not sure can be answered with just a free week under my belt, but as a relatively causal Tarkov Player, I see a truckload of potential within this game, and I really hope they can deliver on that potential with haste and polish. it deserves that at the very least.   I think it warrants at least a try, maybe not a full price tag, but if you're willing to wait for a sale, can definitely be a good experience for people who want a good challenge.
