Pretty solid game.  Looking forward to the "PVE Mode" ect. Shooting mechanics are extremely solid ( Of course, its Bungie.) but time-to-kill is VERY short, meaning that gunfights are fast. This is either good or bad depending on how you like to play things.  Personally, i'd rather have the TTK be a little longer so we could enjoy the tight gunplay. As it stands, it sort of ends up being a  game of "gank or be ganked" because you will never be able to react fast enough to get a shot off on someone else who comes at you from where you dont see them coming.  Unlike Arc Raiders, you dont really get those moments where people chat to each other or have weird social interactions, for whatever reason everyone shoots on sight and does not speak, despite the area chat being present in my experience so far. Again, this is either good or bad. Personally I just feel its "different" which isnt bad, its just a different experience.   As an alternative, it makes the game very risky and keeps tensions high, in that you wont want to engage in fights if you dont absolutely have to and you're constantly playing tactical, which is fun to me. Overall the game IS fun, and the style and graphics are absolutely 10/10, everything is dripping in unique cyberpunk asthetic which is also very cool.   Overall recommend if you enjoy tight shooting and tense gameplay but still has some low risk options ( free loadouts and "rooks" where you are basically an NPC dropped in, which is cool.).  Great to shake things up every once and a while between other multiplayer games.

This game gets a lot of flak what with the whole Destiny 2 end of support thing. But if we stop the infighting and rate the game on it's merits... Here are my thoughts.  Season 1 was interesting, but it was a total slog. Season 2 removes the slog, mostly, but it has other issues. With the introduction of things like the Cradle, and the sponsor kits gifted to players who got a certain level in Season 1, there's already a flood of higher tier gear that completely unbalances things and matchmaking still appears to be off, as it doesn't account for this sort of thing whatsoever. There's also been some server issues since the launch, but they've mostly cleared.  But for those issues, Season 2 does a lot of really cool things. Night Marsh is amazing in the best way. The super low visibility, the need to use a flashlight to see indoors (and sometimes outdoors) and the new scopes that allow you to see better in the dark at closer ranges without giving you thermals are things that add a lot of atmosphere to the bog. Being surprised by runners, or getting into a third party fight with them is exciting and rewarding, but it can also be frustrating and demoralizing when you lose everything, too.  I would give this a solid 7/10, and I recommend trying it (especially during the current free week) and giving it an honest play.

I was already 100 hours into Arc Raiders when I played Marathon's server slam. I played a few hours and wasn't motivated to buy it.   I caught the extraction game bug! Stopped playing Arc Raiders after about 350 hours or so. It was just so boring. Nothing new but cosmetics. So, I jumped into Tarkov, which I LOVE because of its difficulty and intricacies. It's hard to do tasks for traders which keeps you working towards goals.   I jumped into Marathon and it is FANTASTIC now compared to when I first played. You have so many items you gain for your character, which helps keep the game fresh. Load in with faction free kits, extract, get a new skin. I enjoy the factions needing to be unlocked and their contracts. It gives much more depth to the world and more incentive to do runs.   Gunplay is where this shines! It feels fantastic in shootouts. Augments actually feel like they enhance the weapons. I like that you don't exactly level up a gun, you get better attachments. PvP feels even and fair as compared to Tarkov where it's lights out of you have bad gear or Arc Raiders where you're done if you get snuck up on. You're always in for a good fire fight.   The bots are a major threat, which makes you want to bring in great gear or get sneaky. Either way is fun.   Inventory management is refined as compared to Tarkov, which is a slog. You can spend more time in Yarkov working on inventory than going into a raid. Marathon has a much deeper inventory management system than Arc, which is great because you feel like you can really tailor your shells to your play style.   It is kind of a hero shooter, so each shell has special abilities. I enjoy PvP a lot in this game so I use recon to ping enemies nearby and hunt them down. However, there's a shell for pretty much any playstyle.   My one major critique is that there is a lack of unique maps. More maps would make it even more fun. Right now, the maps are fairly easy to traverse and not a lot of diversity.   It's a happy medium between the depth of Tarkov and the shallowness of Arc.   Think Tarkov meets Apex Legends. It is really well optimized and smooth throughout.   I am so bummed it took until now for me to jump in. I still have a lot more to do in the game but the hate it gets is wild compared to what the truth is, which that this game is a very fun extraction shooter that pushes gunplay over player interactions. I can't recommend it enough if you want a fast-paced extraction shooter that is somewhat deep but not too in the weeds. Hop in, get to work on contracts, doing tasks for skins, looting high-tier loot to sell for cradle upgrades, hunt other players for their loot and just good gunplay, and more.

This game is an absolute banger. It’s one of my top three favorite FPS games of the last two decades. The learning curve can be intimidating, but it rewards the time and effort you invest in it. It doesn’t hand you instant gratification. The wins and loot are hard-earned and always fleeting. Gear changes hands frequently, but the meta progression is significantly improved in season two and never feels overly punishing as long as you prioritize completing contracts and entering each run with purpose. A few thoughts on pain points people are experiencing. The game cannot be fully experienced solo. I played much of season one solo and am above average at the game, but the only real endgame content is Cryo (and now the upper complex on Night Marsh). Neither can be soloed. That said, I had great success overall doing crew runs for Cryo last season. Sometimes, you get bodied, but that’s true even with coordinated teams. It’s an endgame PvP map, and it can be brutal, but that’s to be expected in my opinion.  I wish the game had tuning specifically for solo players, even if it was challenging. It would make consistently tackling endgame content easier and more rewarding. I didn’t play the game excessively and am not in the top tier of players, but I still managed to complete four Cryo vaults, four compiler clears, and accumulate a lot of gold by the end of season.  Loot and progression is way better this season and feels much more fair and rewarding without needing to invest streamer level time into the game.  I believe the game receives a lot of unfair criticism. It’s already fantastic, punishing, and incredibly rewarding for anyone who loves PvP, especially those who appreciate the Destiny/Bungie flair of it. The team has been responsive and aware of the pain points players are experiencing and has made changes that make me optimistic that they can continue improving the game over time and turn it into something truly special.

Coming off the heels of the death of Destiny 2, Bungie somehow managed to make this hail mary work. The game is interesting in both the world design and character/npc designs. The gunplay is Bungie gunplay, you'll know what that means if you played any Halo or Destiny game (it's fantastic). Economy can be weird sometimes as they figure out their footing. PvE was a recent addition which only helped to revitalize the game a bit, because players want to explore the world of Marathon, but without fighting other players. That's fine. If you're a PvP/Crucible sweat, then you will find this game enjoyable. If you're someone who likes PvE, well they got stuff for you too. If you're into customization and making your own builds, there are TONS of build crafting options, especially with the release of The Cradle feature (just stat points you can spec to get specific perks and stats).  In-terms of the graphics, I am in awe every time I boot it up. Granted, they still have some rough spots (it is running on an old engine after all), but Bungie knows lighting and particle effects better than most game studios. Those two ingredients combined with Bungie's Sci-Fi worldbuilding prowess just makes for a beautiful game you can really get immersed in. Also for all you nerds who care about lore, yes, there is a TON of codex entries to read (ala books in destiny 2, Grimoires from Destiny 1, Terminals from Halo).   This game has a unique feel and look that no other game captures quite like it. The social aspect of the game is not at all the same as ARC Raiders, but there are times you can have some great interactions. You gotta remember though that this game is a more hardcore version of the extraction shooter genre. Think Tarkov but toned down a little and without the bloated economy system. There is an economy present, but honestly it's nothing important to worry about. You just play the game naturally and you will increase the base level of your kit for future runs, and it doesn't feel like you are ever 'behind' due to the amount of loot you get. Gear fear is not something you have in the game if that helps you decide (unlike ARC raiders where you hoard golds).  One thing I think this game does amazing is allow you to grab most of the weaponry in different tiers. You can find the same grey AR you've used since hour 1 as a gold version later on. This is because the weapon itself gets it's rarity based on the mods you equip to it and their collective value. So you can literally turn that grey AR into a blue within a matter of minutes playing. It's really nice to see those implementations so that everyone can simply use the guns they like...unlike another game on the market where you practically run greys all the time...  Oh one thing to mention is that there are high loot maps as well, but they are on rotation. The ranked playlists are too. It's kinda weird to get used to playing certain maps only on weekends but once you do your first run, you'll see why it was designed this way (think Trials of Osiris and it's great loot from the lighthouse if you go flawless). The map even has a raid boss for the toughest of challenges. I have yet to do it, but I have heard of many people loving the encounter, and the amount of great loot you get.  Anyways, my scores are below for the TL;DR:  Gameplay - 8/10 Graphics - 8/10 Atmosphere - 10/10 Gunplay - 10/10 Customization - 7/10 (was a 6 but they added Cradle, still need better skins on the guns and shells) Maps - 7/10 (confusing layouts for newcomers but it lends into the world-building) Community - 6/10 (take D2 players and put them in an extraction shooter...yah...) Ranked - 1/10 (lol what is this system, it needs some tweaking)  Overall - 7/10 - If you are questioning if the game will be right for you, definitely check it out on free weeks, or buy it on sale.   Other than that, see you in Tau Ceti.

Despite everything going against it, including the series genre change, art plagiarism debacle and the multiple development iterations, everything that should work against Marathon ends up strengthening it. The detached and esoteric lore (which is directly connected to some semi-obscure FPS games from the dawn of the genre) ends up being way more digestible than any infodump seasonal cutscenes would be, the art and music is strange and striking enough to make them hard to ignore, and the extraction-hero shooter mechanics, which are way less newbie friendly than some of Marathon's contemporaries, end up showcasing Bungie's excellent gunplay and slick UI design that make their games so addicting. It ends up making what should feel like very opposing design, story, and aesthetic decisions into something more cohesive than most other AAA live service games of it's ilk. Turning a tried and true genre into something new and strange, funnily enough, feels about as old-school Bungie as it can get.

I've played Rust, 5+ battle royales, a little bit of Tarkov, and other countless FPS titles across many years. I'm the hardcore audience this game was supposed to cater to. I liked ARC Raiders and I think Marathon made a grave mistake by targeting the hardcore extraction shooter audience. ARC Raiders simultaneously proves that "extraction" can work great if it's more casual oriented. Look at their numbers. Only a small fraction of the ARC audience moved to Marathon?  So far I see those issues: - matchmaking queue times aren't great, you can wait minutes just to die in 30 seconds of fast paced match - seems like the game was deliberately designed to reduce gear fear to zero so stakes are low despite genre - various weird gadgets instead of focusing more on aim and positioning - abilities negate ambushes, ttk negates the economy xd  It feels weirdly similar to Apex Legends but has the extraction game mode slapped onto it for no good reason.  Free weekend until 11 Jun I will probably try it more. It's pretty good when it's free (or on sale). The base value of the game is too high to receive a negative review IMHO. Honestly it's more of a wasted potential than a bad game per se. Perfectly fixable with future updates and rebalances.

So this game gets a lot of unnecessary hate as far as i can tell.. i've had it on my wishlist for a while and now its had a free weekend i've finally given it a try. i've been playing games for over 30+ years and so here's my review..  The game is excellently made lets get that straight, its very well polished and designed, i've yet to experience anything that i could say is buggy or doesnt work properly. I think the attention span of people in this day and age is somewhat low and there's a vast amount of story in the game which fits in neatly about how players respawn and their interaction with the world building.  The game feels closer to a say borderlands 2 (but BL3 mayhem level 10) with the fighting of robots etc, there are certainly some similarities with games such as Apex legends (shields, health), solo,duo,trios, and the parkour is not as fluid as say apex legends, there is some of course and some of the characters have abilities to fly and use grappling hooks.  The game is unapologetically hard and that's probably for good reason, the robots are smart enough to take you out if you're not paying attention and add a lovely bit of chaos when there's other human players in the map, there's an insane amount of things to find and use in maps but they're not overwhelming easy to find all the time, ammo shields and health are sparse. the game expects you to play it more like counter strike 2 than say Apex legends.  I found the more i played it the more i liked it, it being an extraction shooter means there is indeed tons of replayability as even the same map can be repeatedly played over with unlimited objectives. and not all the objectivies means you have to engage with other players. as for PVE there are numerous things on maps that can attack or kill you. I would have liked to have seen a Quad mode of some sort even if it was just PVE only.. too many games out there have solo,duos,trios..  I would say give this game a try while you have a chance, there is a bit of a learning curve to get into it, but the more you play it the better it gets and you understand more of what things do. time will tell if i actually purchase the game or not.. it is indeed a good game, the reviews don't lie..

Having sunk dozens of hours into Marathon, it is clear that Bungie has built a deeply compelling, mechanical masterpiece. The game is exceptionally well crafted, boasting fluid movement, tense extraction loops, and a striking visual aesthetic that sets it apart from anything else on the market. The gunplay is tight, and the risk-reward tension of every drop keeps the gameplay loop highly addictive.  However, the game is not without its flaws. While the foundation is rock solid, it still needs a few crucial tweaks to fully realize its potential:  PVE/PVP Balance: Weapon tuning and economy scaling need immediate adjustments to prevent a stale meta from solidifying.  Matchmaking and Stability: Server performance and matchmaking queues require optimization to handle high-level, competitive drops smoothly.  UI/UX Refinements: Menu navigation and in-raid inventory management can feel cluttered during high-intensity moments.  Overall, Marathon is an incredible tactical extraction shooter that respects the player's time through its deep mechanics. With just a few quality-of-life adjustments and balance patches, it could easily become a definitive genre leader.  Rating: 8.5/10

Okay, update!  This is a very beautiful, super weird, and kind of frustrating game. But its good, and fun, and gets more fun the more you play it and experiment. It is still grindy, but the way the game progresses makes the grind feel rewarding instead of a slog. This game also DOES have a story, its just given to you in small pieces as you complete contracts. the WHOLE GAME revolves around completing contracts, and the BIGGEST REWARDS are from completing contracts.  Tips for easy beginning play while solo: Try out each class. I had the most success solo-running with the assassin. You can not complete contracts with the rook, so don't bother until later when you need to farm resources. Frag grenades are very good, and so are smoke grenades. Bubble Shields are gold, and on group runs you should always have one for when you need to exfil. On perimeter, only 3 other players will spawn into the map while on solo-play. There seems to be a minimum distance they must spawn away from you, so you can sprint to your contract objective. The longer the match goes on, the higher the chances that other players have exfilled or died already. If you wait until the last 10 minutes to exfil, and there are multiple locations to do so, look into the sky. If you see a giant beam coming from the ground, go elsewhere; a player activated that exfil. You can try and kill them, but they are literally looking out for danger and have the home advantage.  Focus on upgrading your shell, and getting the freebies unlocked in each faction. The first three factions you unlock are the best for freebies; free credits, free shields, free backpacks, and free healing items.  Once you unlock group play, you can not go wrong with recon, triage, and sentinel.  Thief and Assassin work really well together, as thief can x-ray through assassin smoke, allowing for great battlefield control.  This game has "seasons". This is a boon, and a curse. The boon is that new content will regularly be available over the lifetime of the game. The curse is that this game has a shelf-life, and each season the devs may reset some abilities. Play it while its around if you want to play this game, because if it doesn't have players the game dies. Once bungie moves on to other projects, this game will most likely be sun-downed. Saw the game was free for the weekend so decided to give it a go. Have been a huge fan of Bungie's past works (Halo, OG Marathon, etc) and I have to say, I am disappointed. I have been playing solo, and yes I am aware that playing duos or trios is widely considered to be less punishing and more in line with true PvP combat, but the game is built around being a rat. I will say though, I do love the cyber/digital Y2K style the game is going for. It makes the environments, weapons, and even UI very unique and distinct, and the contracts with the factions helps with the immersion as Bungie finger-feeds us with lore and story through them. The lore in Bungie games, I find, has always been interesting and I think the studio has refined how they hint at events in a very subtle and intriguing way. Overall, a fine game, with some critical flaws.

Marathon is conditionally fun. The parts it does well, it does incredibly so - the trio PvP experience, the gunplay, the faction rank and upgrade system, and the visual design are all top notch. I mean truly top of their class in these regards.    The issues are the conditions you have to meet to get to enjoy that.  - The solo experience is nightmarish and heavy on invisible extraction campers.  - The seasonal wipe makes it hard to enjoy Marathon; if it is not the only game you play, you will not get to experience some of the later faction rewards, which are game changers. The game asks for a heavy commitment to get the full experience.  - Marathon requires being fully-alert and on-edge. This is not a bad thing in itself - it is very fun when you're in the mood for something like that. However, if you come home tired and want to just relax in its world, there is no way to do that.    I am hopeful that Bungie continues to expand Marathon's world/gunplay into new modes - ones for those who aren't looking for a constant high-tension PvP experience that resets, but still enjoy what Marathon offers outside of that. I still recommend the game to those who are looking for that PvP thrill though.

Marathon Review – A Game That Grew on Me  I'll be honest: when I first tried Marathon, I didn't like it.  Part of that was my own expectations. I originally thought it was going to be a massive 100-player experience with nonstop action and a completely different style of extraction gameplay. When I jumped in for the first time, it didn't click with me at all, and I walked away pretty disappointed.  During the recent free play period, though, I decided to give it one more chance.  After spending a few more hours with the game, learning the mechanics, understanding the extraction loop, and actually engaging with some of the PvE content, something finally clicked. Once I stopped trying to play it like the game I expected and started playing it for what it actually is, I found myself having a pretty good time.  The gunplay feels solid, the tension of extracting with valuable loot creates some genuinely exciting moments, and the PvE encounters add more variety than I initially gave the game credit for. There is definitely a learning curve, and I think that's where a lot of new players may bounce off. The game doesn't immediately explain why its gameplay loop is satisfying—you have to spend some time with it before it starts making sense.  My biggest complaint is the wipe system. I understand why faction progression, inventory, and vault contents need to be reset periodically to keep the economy healthy and maintain a level playing field. However, I wish there was some form of permanent progression that stayed with your account. Even something as simple as a persistent character level, career rank, or prestige system would give players something to show off between wipes and make long-term investment feel more rewarding.  Overall, Marathon ended up surprising me. It's not the game I thought it was going to be, but once I adjusted my expectations and learned how it wants to be played, I found a lot to enjoy.  Score: 7.5/10  Not a game that instantly hooks everyone, but if you're willing to learn its systems and give it some time, there's a genuinely fun extraction shooter underneath the rough first impression.

Marathon is a really fascinating and fun take on the extraction shooter genre. I really enjoy the swings they are taking mechanically and artistically. I think they have some of the best AI enemies in this genre, with very complex behaviors. The guns all feel good and varied just like Halo and Destiny before this. And there is a lot of loot to be had.    The one area this game far exceeds it's peers is the raid style encounters that Bungie has designed with multi step operations, puzzles, enemy encounters, and mini-bosses. So if you find yourself drawn to that type of later game objective gameplay this has it in spades. I only wished they had a bit more tutorializing of the puzzle boxes, so you had a natural ramp through the game to them. Really you need to either have a dedicated squad or watch Youtube videos to really be able to tackle these.    I think the area where Marathon is weakest right now is its readability and entry level content. The game is quite difficult to parse even for someone like me who has hundreds of hours in Arc Raiders, and Tarkov, and really knows the genre. They need to do a pass on the loot icons and the way the UI functions for speed and utility. So if you can get over that initial hurdle I think there is a lot to love about this game, especially as they build up the more PvE modes. As the game is there is only Night Marsh Sponsored for a really focused PvE mode and everything else is PvP as the baseline, so be aware of that, and maybe wait till they build up the PvE before jumping in if that is something you prioritize.

Marathon felt like a disappointing experience to me. Even though Bungie tried to present it as a fresh take on the extraction shooter genre, the reality is that the game feels empty and unrewarding. From the very beginning, the learning curve is unnecessarily harsh, and there’s no clear system to help players adapt. Instead of motivating me to keep playing, it left me frustrated and drained.  The multiplayer, which should be the heart of the game, is poorly balanced. Playing solo is practically impossible, and the heavy reliance on coordinated teams makes the game feel exclusionary. This breaks the idea of accessibility and leaves out those who want a more flexible experience.  Another major issue is the monetization. The microtransactions and battle pass system feel abusive, with repetitive and uninspiring rewards. It gives the impression that the business model was prioritized over fun, turning every match into a reminder that progress is limited more by market decisions than by player skill.  On the technical side, the interface is confusing and overloaded, making it hard to understand the game’s systems. Progression is slow and unsatisfying, leaving me with the impression that no matter how much time I invest, advancement will always be minimal.  In conclusion, Marathon doesn’t live up to Bungie’s legacy. Rather than being a worthy successor to their classic franchise, it feels like a failed experiment that put monetization ahead of quality. While ambitious in concept, it fails to deliver a solid or recommendable experience..

Also it's 2026 can we PLEASE have a maximum fov of 120 and NO fov scaling when sprinting? for this game its fuggsin nauseating, not so much with Destiny which is pretty much exactly the same but there's commonly a horizon in your view where as this game you're clearing buildings constantly. I enjoy that there is a camaraderie about Arc Raiders when you're helping players kill the Arc bots, and the slight tension of distrust when a player you help approaches you. I've been thinking through the criticisms and praise I could fairly give the game, and ultimately it's hard to pin it down. Why? because Marathon is not a fun game, but it's not a poorly made one. There are some things I wish were better, like the audio being more recognizable and informative at a glance, also easier to learn (Arc does really well at this so it's hard not to compare), or the AWFUL time to kill, but it makes for a miserable experience. The UI has improved a bit from the Network Test, but it's still not great and the visuals can be retina destroying. looting feels less satisfying than Arc IMO since you mostly just sell ♥♥♥♥ on exfil. Stuff is used for faction upgrades, but that feels less compelling than using stuff to make stuff. Also the Armor mechanics are less interesting than arc, and enemies with better shields or snipers are a nightmare.  I think the low TTK makes for a very compelling feeling of vulnerability and like "hesitation is defeat", where any wrong move could spell disaster for the enemy or you, but it's not my cup of tea. It's not fun getting one shot by sniper dudes with purple shields already.  [h2]My advice?[/h2] if you don't like low TTK shooters where you're dead in either a single shot or within .5 seconds, do NOT touch this game. I don't prefer this TTK in a game like this, I prefer it in siege bc the gameplay revolves around it entirely, but here it feels like ♥♥♥♥. Lose everything to a stray bullet. Also I don't think the game's loops are as compelling as its competitors, nor do I like that the story is told my looping .mp4s that talk at you. I know people say the story is super cool or they like the vibe, but it feels pretty lazy to me. Better is the environmental storytelling, I'm not reading the codex lol.

To start with Marathon is the second game in the Extraction Shooter genre I've played. The first one was Tarkov but that was when that game first came out. Played it for a while and it just wasn't for me. Sold the account. Marathon free week was a great opportunity to try a game that is being touted by its fans as the best Extraction Shooter. As background I have thousands of hours on steam in different type of shooters. I have some experience in certain shooter mechanics and gameplay. The positives: The actual gun play and mechanics are really good. You can clearly see this is a Bungie game. Guns feel different and unique. The movement and abilities are good and polished. This is the best aspect of the game. Clearly worked on and polished to the max. Environment and the overall feel of the different zones is good as well. I like the sounds, weather effects and different biomes you encounter. The sound design and overall feel is also great. The “Shells” or the actual characters you play have decent abilities and feel different to play. The negatives: The UI of the menus on overall experience in going through different menus is good awful. It just looks terrible and sometimes looking for a specific things is truly tedious. The art style of the game and the style of the characters is just boring and doesn't fit my personal aesthetic. I think with time this will age even worse. Models look like someone took the “Concord” characters and made them more “futuristic”. Don't like the design or the look. The grind for the different factions is just utterly pointless and boring. It feels like this was designed as a free to play model with a paid incentive to speed it up. Also the fact all of this is resetting every season and you have to do it again is a stupid design decision. How the gun look as well is in my opinion bad. Square blocks with strange ergonomics and one colour slapped on a side. The guns are just boring. There is no rule of cool effect with the guns. Skins... My god you want to unlock the colour blue. Play for hours and do one odd specific thing in game to unlock the colour blue. You want a slightly different light blue pay. This is the second part of the game that feels as a f2p design. Normally if you like the game and want to spend some extra money to help the developer the skins most often have the “cool effect”. The skins are just bad. The prices in the game shop are also crazy. You want a bundle of pink skins you need to buy the in game currency that doesn't match the bundle price. So first you pay more and have currency leftover that you can't do northing with. This isn't just Bungie it's every developer but it's just anointing as hell. Bungie could have done something for the players but they didn't. Game stability is good but I had 5 crashes playing Marathon. I one of the I did have a decent amount of times and loot which I was annoyed I've lost. There should be some option maybe to restart the run with that gear and have another go?  Overall I do not recommend this game. This feels in certain parts like a f2p game you have to buy first to play. I will not be buying it after the free period ends on the 11th of June. If this was a f2p game it would probably be in my rotation of games I play on and off. But I'm not going to spend even the 20£ for this. In my opinion in the next 6 months this game does f2p. Wait for that you're not missing anything anyway because of the resets.

I look at Marathon with Grief and despair.  Even after 18hours of playing, marathon to me is a mostly disappointing experience. I'm a sucker for the Art Direction and Trailers, but the way the game feel is complete miss compared to what I was hoping for after seeing all the promo material, which makes this disappointment all the more painful and frustrating knowing I'm not going to be able to enjoy the world because it's trapped behind a game experience I don't like.   After playing for a while you get used to the mechanics, but this game requires you to put a good 5 hours AT LEAST for it to make itself understood, before that you will get shot, killed, stabbed, stolen from without any lesson to learn from these experiences. It will give you no enjoyment at all for your first day of playing and I think that's why so many people have a negative impression of the game.   To me, Marathon fails in giving the new player something nice to play with outside of the experience of killing / getting killed during the first 5 hours. For newcomers : the looting is absolutely incomprehensible and uneventful, the UI is still a maze, the movement is really stiff, stamina is very restrictive, the 1st 2 maps are very similar and passable in terms of design, all interiors look the same, PVE is bullets and heals tax without much reward.  Here's everything I would change for newcomers :  -MOVEMENT :  Should have had fun movement, give players a bit more air strafing or a bit of B-Hop to carry momentum.  -CAMERA :  The game feels very flat compared to most FPS, allow players to increase their FOV past 105. Guns are bulky and take a lot of screen-space, allow players to have some control over their View-model, make it stand further away from the POV or lower. -MAPS & LEVEL DESIGN :  Perimeter and Dire Marsh are too similar. Perimeter looks rather ugly, rocks and grass come together inorganically, POIs feel unorganized and copy pasted throughout. The game would've benefited a lot from the first 2 maps selling the range of what marathon's Art Direction allows them to do as we saw in the trailers. Dire Marsh should've had these milky ponds, keeping the green / blue for itself while Perimeter should've had a warmer, orange / red theme to it, perhaps like the canyons or beach we saw in Alberto Mielgo's cinematic short for the game. This game would also benefit a lot from a shooting range where you can get familiar to all the game's weapons and mods, a safe space for the new player to try what the game has to offer, maybe with a couple locked behind doors to make them want to unlock them. -HOSTILES & PVE :  The spores and the red goo HL2 spiders in their current design have no place in this game imo, they really feel like uninspired, cheap copy paste enemies that bring more frustration to the new player than it adds wonder or character to the world. The UESC designs are fine, but the different types are undistinguishable from one another if you're new to the game. Without training, and a free kit, you're fighting Bullet & Heal IRS, without a quick way to associate one silhouette to a tier of robot danger, even their colours are hard to understand. There's also little feedback for the player to understand how much damage they've dealt. I think a good example for PVE done right is, again, Embark's other game. Where enemies have obvious but not cheap weak points, engaging to aim for, clear feedback on what damage you deal. Every enemy feels like its own puzzle. Marathon's PVE interactions generally dumb down to a cookie clicker feel of if you can avoid enough bullets, have enough heals and enough bullets and can shoot for long enough. LOOTING :  This should be full of interactions such as the ones when you're typing and hacking but for looting. Safe hacking, hammering a box, sound queues on legendaries and mythics, animations off pulling drawers, taking harddrives out of a computer, pulling boxes from under the table, not just random items lying around on shelves and "press E longer" to open weapon crate. Embark's other game does it incredibly well. Looting all in itself should be an exciting, interactive experience the new player should be able to grasp and enjoy in his first hours of playing. POST-PROCESSING & EFFECTS : Marathon has this glow / diffusion wash on all the time on bright surfaces that makes me feel like I've got my camera oily or glasses dirty. When you shoot at an enemy, PVE or PVP , it's so hard to keep track of them as a new player because of all the gross and bulky shield break effects, sparks, flares and glows, on top of already bulky scopes and sights. As you play more you get used to it but it's not welcoming, it feels messy and confusing.   One last gripe before I post this. This game is about people in 3D printed shells, there's close to nothing as creatively freeing as 3D printing, let me play with the shells like a customizable Bionicle. You can keep the main runners as a base for new players to have a quick entry into the game with readymade characters, but what a waste it is having such a cool world to end up with a hero shooter, let others customize their abilities and specs. Let me play dress to impress, GIVE ME ACCESS TO THE PRINTER, I want to be able to build my own hero, and that will give players more reasons to spend on cosmetics and to play the game as you're now letting me have some creative control in the game.  Marathon isn't a bad game, it's a very good game for a very small amount of players and a terrible experience for most. If Bungie wants to get a mass appeal, they can, they just need to make the experience better for a lot more people and not just for a niche. Marathon's problem isn't marketing, they had huge coverage, it's how the game compares to the marketing, the game is rather ugly and tame compared to the short film and its first trailer. I want Marathon to succeed as I love the world and I'm completely hooked on it, but I just can't play the game as it is right now. It's just so meh and frustrating to see such a cool world go to "waste" on a game feel like this one. To me, it really feels like the game design and the art direction are 2 different worlds that don't come together well. This is actual grief to me.  Anyhow, try it out, see how it feels, I don't want it to crash and burn, I want bungie to make it better and convince people before Sony decides to pull the plug. I'm in despair seeing such a cool world and universe drift away in a boat full of hubris. It's real Grief for me to look at it and having to tell it goodbye knowing I probably will not ever see Marathon in its absolute slam dunk form the way it should've been.

First and probably last time playing Marathon.  The first two matches were enough for me. I had no idea where to go, what I was supposed to do, or even what my objectives were. The game does a terrible job of introducing new players to its mechanics.  Movement feels sluggish and unresponsive compared to other extraction shooters. It honestly feels like a slower version of Apex Legends, but without the fluidity that makes Apex enjoyable.  The UI is a mess. The main menu is confusing, the inventory management feels poorly designed, and finding basic information takes more effort than it should.  The loading screens are awful. Watching the same insect crawl into someone's mouth over and over again is more disgusting than interesting, and the loading music became irritating almost immediately.  I've played extraction shooters like Delta Force and Escape from Tarkov, and both felt far more intuitive despite being complex games. Marathon just felt chaotic and directionless from the start.  Maybe the game gets better after several hours, but a game shouldn't require players to fight the interface and figure out what they're supposed to be doing before it becomes enjoyable.  Not recommended.

It was my first extraction shooter, and thus it's hard to judge, but I'll try. I usually play games for the experience, and appreciate any cosmetic or numerical rewards as a bonus, I feel like that is the exact opposite of what this game is going for.  The good is that it runs like a dream even on a potato, it needs a CPU more than a GPU, pleasant surprise. The colors remind me of Mirror's Edge almost, in the best way.  The bad is that it's kind of... dull. Beyond the strong artstyle and flashy colors, the gameplay loop is both inherently disappointing to me and further worsened by the playerbase.  You can do two things, rat or fight. Ratting is pretty predictable, you drop down, fight a bunch of AI damage sponges, and leave with what you can find. Easy way to farm loot, if a little slow.  Fighting, something that usually seeks you rather than being sought, sucks. If you're caught by surprise or while ratting, nothing you do really matters. The TTK is HORRIBLY LOW, by the time you turn around 2 of your friends are dead. And no, you can't revive them, because EVERY SINGLE PLAYER will camp their bodies until the game ends. This community sucks, nobody uses proximity chat either.  What's the point of a game with social aspects that never see use? The PVE is not enough to compensate for how boring and one-dimensional the PVP is.  And jumping people isn't that satisfying either, The guns feel amazing, but it's hard to enjoy them when fights are over so fast. It's like someone took everything that sucked about Apex and made it into a new game. I didn't like Apex very much either.  You do keep whatever you can find, and there are character upgrade trees but I... don't really care about "number go up". A game is either fun or it isn't, the numbers attached to it don't mean much without good gameplay. Most of my matches consisted of never seeing another person, or getting immediately domed.  I don't like how harsh this review turned out, the game IS very well done, but everything that appealed to me was outweighed by what didn't.

Was interested in Marathon because, Bungie. The gameplay felt heavy and slow, but the shooting felt pretty good. The lore seems super interesting as you learn about the different factions and their objectives, the "shells" and the technology behind it, and the sci-fi lore of Tau Ceti IV shared by your digital handler ONI. My biggest gripe with this game though is, it is really hard to do a solo run. And I found it extremely annoying how stingy the game is with ammo! I can be in the middle of a run and then run out of ammo because the enemies take like more than half a clip to kill, and now I'm screwed, yay fun gameplay (-_-). The game does not hold your hand so you have to learn fast otherwise the game may feel overwhelming to you or you could feel lost on what to do. I usually play solo and I thought I could solo this game but, the map designs, enemies, weak loadouts/weapons/gear, lackluster loot while looting and after-run rewards, made it a frustrating experience and where the game fell short for me. This game has potential to be something great, but if you plan to give this game a try, definitely have a friend group with you or join a crew with randos because the gear you have early game is definitely not suitable for solo runs. I'd say still check it out and see for yourself but, for me, I think I have had my "fun" and will pass on Marathon :(

I'll give my review.  I'm 51/49, or maybe 60/40, or maybe 65/35 for not recommended/recommended.  –  Intro comments. I think it's a great game and it's worth playing, but, this extraction shooter style of grinding, looting - it's just not what I'm looking for.  I love PvP. But this PvP is different. I just kill other teams for no reason basically. And things are derailed by people being distracted doing quests, puzzles, ai, etc.  I like to play real competition, not catch people offguard doing other things you have to do in the game, etc. - so none of these things are thrilling or satisfying - but there ARE satisfying fights, definitely.  It can definitely be a lot of fun to interact with the world, fight teams, etc. - but I just can't get with this gameplay loop, which I’ll explore more in the next section.  --  Gameplay loop. I think it breaks down like this. There are 2 well-known types of video games:  1. An adventure game where you progress missions and upgrade your character as the game goes from start to finish. 2. A PvP game where you battle other teams, using strategy, etc., and everyone has equal opportunities.  This style of game is like a mixture of the two. I have to do quests and progress, which takes a lot of game time, like how an adventure game would be. And it's a PvP world and you can always seek PvP, as well as be required to engage when you happen to meet up in a lobby. So this is brilliant, right. A unique mix of the two, very satisfying.  I think many players find enjoyment with that. But here's my feedback:  PLEASE change this system to be better.  This is not good enough. I have to grind these pointless quests and feed the weaveworms loot - this is just busywork, bro. It has no livelihood to it, it has no interesting parts. I either want the thrill of PvP, or the intrigue of an adventure.  This is why I can't recommend the game. I'm not gonna criticize the PvPvE world itself - it has plenty to offer and for humans to experience, compete, have fun - but this implementation is not enough.  These quests are crazy boring and I don't want to be doing useless time waster missions matter transfer after matter transfer.   So you can ask, what's even the point of progressing? Well, to have stronger stats, more access to good loot - so this is satisfying, right. But this isn't enough, we can't just grind stuff over and over just to get this - the grind itself needs to be more satisfying if we are going to force the grind. Adventure games have that satisfying appeal of progressing areas, fighting new enemies, gearing up for the next boss. Here, there is a bit of this with map quests, which is cool, but it’s just not enough. Realistically, there could be WAY more intriguing stuff.  And you might also say that the point is so that you can play PvP - so why wouldn't I just load up a PvP game and get straight to it haha. Again though it is a unique experience where you have progression and you have items of varying impact that you have to buy or loot for while doing PvP, which can provide a satisfying type of fight. But man it's just too much busywork for my book.   I love PvP, and I love intriguing adventures. I don't love needing to grind stuff that's boring busywork (with occasional fun and intriguing moments) while sometimes engaging in PvP. Yeah, idk man.  So I’ll support the genre, but I think it's in a difficult spot. It NEEDS more. This formula is not enough I think. I would like to see it shaken up a ton rather than repeating 90% of the same formula from other games.  I also think you gotta have more maps. These are pretty intricate and interesting, but ideally you would absolutely have more, especially when it’s not a growing free to play game and comes out the gate $40.  --  Worldbuilding. Totally love the other parts of the game. Very cool atmosphere and lore and world. The colors and visuals are amazing. I didn't get too deep into understanding the map designs but they got a lot going on, many details, seems to be well made and this is itself engaging.  To be honest, I don’t much like the designs of the shells, just not my style, but I think they’re cool, too.  I also really like the quotes from the shells about death not being an issue. Lots of great vibes here. And there could be loads more vibes as well! If it’s meant to have features of a $40 adventure game, let’s get it.  –  Cost. So, what are we paying for here with a $40 game? There are many free to play PvP games with maps, gunplay, heroes, lore.  Are we paying for a sideline progression system and these weak quests that just serve as busywork between when I begin playing the game and the PvP fights I get to play later? Is that the $40 value, rivaling an adventure game where you travel places and do interesting things and fight different bosses? (The AI is cool, idk how much that costs, but still mostly a basic repeated thing rather than the uniqueness expected from an adventure game.)  –  Lore issues/shoehorn. The lore is lovely. But it doesn’t make sense with what you are doing constantly in-game. We are humans loading onto this planet, and we can literally just loot it, kill UESC, and extract over and over.   I really don’t care for this. We shouldn’t just shoehorn extraction shooters onto these lores, it’s too awkward. Either have a nonsense situation where there is no explanation and we can lean into that we just PvP for fun, or have a lore that actually gives a reason for why everybody kills on sight.  –  At the end of the day, I’m gonna point out the obvious and say I think you can have way more success with this game’s worldbuilding if it weren’t an extraction shooter.  But again, I don’t want to just flame extraction shooters - there is something super cool and satisfying here.  But there’s too many missing pieces here. Slow grinding of quests and progression that have no intrigue (again the lore is interesting but it’s not enough to just have some dialogue after “push-button-quest”).  The PvP is too dumb sometimes. Yeah, let’s kill on extract, right. Totally pointless since you can’t even loot the body. And this is after a 20 minute session. I don't find it intriguing to waste 20 minutes, and I don't find it intriguing to waste 20 minutes of another player's time. This is a nonexistent issue in other genres and it just feels dumb. Not to mention all the hiding and camping you can do, etc. - but there are a lot of mechanics to keep things churning and I can’t say this is a huge issue.  –  Core feedback. I think if we want extraction shooters to come out and be satisfying to more players, we gotta get some game designers on this and really change all this stuff, do it differently.   That’s my core feedback. Obviously just a random steam review lol, but it was fun.

TLDR: I don't know if I have ever been more on the fence about a game, but ultimately I don't think the juice is worth the squeeze. I would describe Marathon as mostly tedious, with sparse moments of very high highs peppered in. But those moments are hidden behind dozens of splash screens, loading screens, menuing, and waiting to queue. This leaves you feeling bitter at the game's innate difficulty and punishing progression system, when those elements are supposed to be part of its appeal.      Quick background:     I had a few friends convince me to try this for the free week. Out of the 4 of us that tried it, I was the only one who stuck with it for the whole week. I have never played an extraction shooter before. One in our group has played Arc Raiders, but he bounced off of it mid week. I was not really interested in this game at all tbh, and was surprised that I wound up kind of getting glued to it for the week I had it, even watching some content online to understand the maps, events, and systems.     Things I liked:     I really enjoy some parts of the game. The guns are fun. I like the gun mods and the way the rest of the player kits function. There are multiple shells (classes) that each have their own niche, and I feel like there is a good degree of personal expression in the way you can play each one. The other "meta" upgrade systems are a little confusing but you can pretty much not worry about that stuff until a few hours in. Ultimately, once you wrap your head around the progression, it's actually decently structured OUTSIDE of the contract system (which I will talk about below).     Getting the jump on people feels really good, especially if they have better gear than you. That is probably the main attraction for people who keep playing this type of game. And I do want to emphasize that when you actually have a decent team and you wipe a few other teams, or if you get through a couple really dicey fights, the adrenaline is very high.     Once I started understanding some of the PvE events scattered across the map like lockdowns, looting Command Wing in Pinwheel, toxic rooms, etc., the game completely transformed from something kind of mid to being actually really interesting. The events are usually pretty cool, and I like the added element of knowing you are up against genuinely hard bot encounters as well as a team possibly third partying you while you're tied up with the event. I think it would be cool if the devs leaned into this aspect more, or even had bigger set pieces where you are in a strictly PvE environment that then dovetails into a PvP area or something like that. These parts of the game require very tight team play and are genuinely difficult, but are probably the most fun in the game I had. My one criticism of these elements is that they are just not explained very well, or it's not really apparent that there are multiple steps at different locations that have to be done in a certain order, so you usually have to fumble your way through them or look up guides online.     Things I didn't like:     Now for why I ultimately don't recommend Marathon unless you are a very specific type of gamer. The game is extremely sweaty and the time to kill is super quick. I don't mind this in theory, but the amount of time spent loading in and out of matches, adjusting inventory, etc., is just way out of proportion with how much you can actually play the game and have fun. I am a pretty decent FPS player, and otherwise usually enjoy hard/difficult games, but most of my sessions ultimately ended with me just feeling exhausted clicking through the various splash screens and notifications that just go on and on. Run ends, load screen, then multiple splash screens with inventory/xp/salvage info, loading for menu, then you have to tinker with your inventory (or just take a free kit if you're sick of dealing with it), queue up/choose map, MAKE SURE CREW FILL IS ON (for some reason it kept turning this off and I discovered I was solo in a trio lobby), wait to get matched, more loading, and then possibly get killed within 5 minutes just to do it all again.     This is combined with the extremely punishing contract system, where many of the contracts require you to successfully complete a number of objectives around the match and then successfully exfiltrate to advance any of your faction rep (this determines what's available in shops, how much free stuff you get, etc.). This is only made worse if you are queuing with randoms, because you're really rolling the dice with players who don't understand when to not take a bad engagement, or when to push/be aggressive, how to stalk/ambush, etc. Teammates also have their own contracts so it really can just turn into a jogging simulator if you're trying to get everybody's stuff done. My uninstall point was on Selection Pressures 4/4, which I failed repeatedly (solo and trios) before just saying yeah it's not getting done.     And that's about where I am right now. I think if I had homies that were really wanting to get good together I would be more motivated to keep going. But as is, I just don't have the time to get invested at the level that this game requires of me, especially given how much downtime there is and how much friction there is between a run ending and you getting back into a match.

This game is very visually appealing. I love the way it looks and feels. The classes (shells) are fun. Our experience every single run either included dying from much stronger players any time we encountered them (oftentimes ending our run within the first 5 minutes), or hardly encountering anything at all. The result was either boring or frustrating.  I had initially written a whole paragraph about how badly I think the health UI and healing system is designed, but I'll skip to the true issue that encompasses that; this is an extraction shooter that seems to be trying to appeal to casual gamers, but fails. It's a pretty complicated game that, in my experience, struggles to allow new players to  get their foot in the door even if they try.

This is another prove of Talented Devs following bad decisions: - This game is a simple copy of some existent and past Extraction Shooters, they just replicated some mechanics and added a new visual style on it. So that, you won't find something new that you couldn't experience in old games. - The only thing you can rescue from this project is the PvP feeling and Character locomotion. - The loot, economy or progress system are a pure sh...t, you can increase your economy since there are not useful or valuable objects to do so. - The balance between weapons, loot objects, consumables and others isn't working at all, so you can kill an enemy and bring everything back for 2 simple reasons: 1 you won't have space enough in your backpack, 2 you need to decide between consumables or gear if you are planning to make another run, so preferable you will be taking the consumables. - The UI/UX experience is like Minecraft, you won't get familiarized with this game icons unless you have about 3 years playing 24hours a day. - The environment feels like you're in a really poor blockout of any indie videogame. - And the last thing, they just forgot this is an Extraccion Shooter, where you will lose more than 50% of your runs, so that you need a Safe Pocket to save something valuable and keep your economy healthy.  PD: I've played alpha, beta, free week test and they keep presenting the same sh...t, nothing new at all.

(Also jesus ♥♥♥♥ imagine having a 40$ game and still trying to milk players with poorly executed premium battlepasses grow the ♥♥♥♥ up).  Ontop of these glaring issues the AI has some really odd bugs that are just completely absurd that they passed playtest. I decided to try out this game after hearing it was going to be free to play for a week. I was pretty excited to play this as I thought it'd be a cool game to play.  [b]FYI: I only played about 2 hours before deciding to not continue. Which is a shame but I'll get to it.[/b]   The visuals are great but the intro was confusing and it happened so fast that I lost track of what was going on. The gameplay is okayish. It was easy to understand although I hate that it's basically Minecraft Hardcore once you die. I died twice in the tutorial and then ended up forfeiting it by accident. Turns out you can't replay it if you do skip so that sucks.   From there I just didn't know what else to do in the game. I never got to understand what Exfil meant or what you were even supposed to do in the mission other than basically do a supply run. Then when you get shot/stunned, you're not able to do anything other than Give Up?? Wtf. There might've been something in the tutorial that I missed because I didn't know what to do here.  I get that the game is supposed to be super hard and I lack experience with FPS shooters (outside a certain game that shall not be named 💀) but this was brutal man. I don't recommend this to anyone who  don't have any experience with extract shooters or are simply trying to shoot enemies and players for fun. Once you die you basically lose everything and it quickly becomes infuriating.   I'd rate this a 2/10 if I could. I'm not saying this is a bad game by the way. It's very beautiful and the gameplay was fun (until I got shot and died lmao). It just wasn't for a noobie like me :/ I might reconsider this game if Bungie allowed us to replay the tutorial. But I have high doubts about that ever happening.   This is Blue. Signing off.

Look, I know this negative review is just one of many that are being posted in response to Destiny getting it's final update, but I want to make one thing clear:  There IS a fun, challenging, PvP game here. I would not have spent 80+ hours on this game if there wasn't something appealing about it and I wish it could stand along something like Arc Raiders.  However, that fun is offset by a number of stupidly hardcore design choices that poke and prod your patience until it inevitably wears you down and you decide it's not worth spending the effort playing anymore.  What problems am I talking about? Well, I could rant and rave about many issues, but I like to focus this review on two particular missing features:  [h3]No practice range/mode[/h3]  While Arc Raider's practice range has its limitations and could be improved upon, the fact that it has a SAFE PLACE you can go test out a new loadout was tremendous in helping me get over my initial gear fear. At least I had some idea as to how many body shots I'd need to down a raider if I needed to defend myself. Meanwhile in Marathon, I found an exotic mag mod for the overrun auto rifle that turned it into a burst-fire rifle that I [b]never[/b] used as I didn't know how effective it was. If there was a practice range, I could have used it there to see if it was worth bringing out on a run or just sell it for some credits, but I ended up just letting it rot away in my vault.  [h1] NO SAFE POCKETS [/h1] This is something the hardcore henrys of this game will fight against, but I'm going to firmly stress this point: extraction shooters suck if you spend so much time in a match, only to get killed at the very end, lose everything, and gain NOTHING for it. There was a match I had in Arc Raiders where I looted some rubber ducks, destroyed some Arc, looted their cores, said hi to some fellow raider, went to loot a cache...and then got downed and knocked out by that same raider. You know what I thought after that happened? I thought "At least I put those Arc parts and my rubber duckies in my safe pockets!". Mind you, I lost some pretty good weapons and gear, but just HAVING those safe pockets does WONDERS in making players like myself feel somewhat better after a loss. If Bungie wants me back, they need to add safe pockets, full stop. I don't care if they start with some limited version of safe pockets (i.e. salvage-only safe pockets, only available if your loadout is above a certain monetary value), that alone would entice me to come back to try the game again.  I don't want Bungie to go away. They are a prestigious studio who still know how to make firing guns in their games still feel great. However, if they are going to be focusing their efforts entirely on Marathon, they need to lay off on some of the annoying hardcore elements of their game. Granted, it might not be enough to save it, but it's better than doing nothing, dammit!

[h1] Marathon Review [/h1]  I wanted to give my honest opinion about this game. I will most likely keep this short, sweet and to the point.  If you like Extraction Shooters and you are wanting to play one that is currently still being supported (at the time of writing this review), Play Marathon.  The gun play in ANY Bungie game has and always will feel amazing, I cannot fault them on that. Bungie and how the weapons feel and sound have always been their number one thing that they have gotten right when it comes to them making games, so... Why dont I like this game?  Its an Extraction Shooter. I have always disliked Extraction Shooters and already feel so burnt out with how many I have seen over the recent few years. You have DMZ (Call of Duty), Arc Raiders, Escape from Tarkov, Hunt: Showdown, Dark and Darker.  Dont get me wrong, im sure the game play loop is exactly what some people want and I will not fault them for that, its just not for me. I have tried, all the games I have listed are games I have played (including this one) and I just don't like these types of games. I feel so disheartened when I want to load into a game with some mid-tier loot and have a little fun and a laugh and then suddenly im POPPED IN THE HEAD by some guy who has figured out that you can slide cancel grapple jump just to make me see GOD on the other side of his shotgun he probably picked up 10 minutes ago. I DONT FIND THAT FUN, IM SORRY  In any came, great for extraction shooter players, horrible for people who just wanted to have a little run around and be a little goofy.  Also give us Destiny 3 I guess as well, idk

What it does well: Graphically it's fantastic, the gunplay is consistently good, the art style is unlike anything else out right now and the map design lends itself to sharp, energetic moments of combat that is exceptionally driving.  Why I'm not recommending it: There's not really a whole lot to justify the price as a live service game.   Of the four maps that are currently available, while incredibly detailed, they quickly wear through their novelty and the game then starts to devolve into the archetype of every other extraction shooter on the market. The behavioral sink of players adhering to certain metas alongside the limited selection of environments makes it so that it was exceedingly easy to burn through what accessible content there was. Likewise, the game arbitrarily restricts what times certain content becomes accessible which further compounds player burnout. What little story the game does have at this point are told through text entries in the codex and through environmental storytelling (which I begrudgingly admit I found deeply immersive) but if their prior projects are any indication, major developments on that front are quite a long while off.   Adjacent to all of this, the terms of service force you to sign a waiver stipulating that you can't participate in any class action lawsuits filed against Bungie for ANY reason. This is highly irregular for most software licensing agreements (doubly so since it's a game) and I'm somewhat disconcerted keeping it on my machine with that in mind.  (Received for free by virtue of the free playtest period in June 2026)

With Destiny being ended and the free to play weekend running, I figured I'd give it a go. I am a Destiny lover, but I went into this with an open mind, and tried to keep my bias to a minimum. That said, if this is what Bungie killed Destiny for, then Bungie is doomed.   Cons:  It's an extraction shooter. If you don't like extraction shooter, you will not like this game.   The massive info dump at the start. I have zero clue what was said during that info dump cause there was waaaayyy too much going on.   Quest progress is very difficult. I had 10 runs to try and find this Commander bot. Never saw them. Waste of time.  The general art style. I get that it's supposed to feel like a half build colony, but a pallet that says pallet on it just feels lazy and breaks my immersion.   You get dumpstered by people so fast. Every time I died to a player, I never even saw them. I had zero warning, zero chance to defend myself, and zero actual gun fights with other player. I would heard shooting, see the damage arrows, and be dead before I could even react.  You can say skill issue all you want, the TTK is still way too fast.   The loading screens are creepy. Very subject I know, but I found the loading screens into runs to be bizarre to the point I couldn't look at them. They made my skin crawl.  It took forever to find a match. Wait times are 5+ minuets. Way to long. I could find a Crucible match and be half way done with it in the time it takes to find a Marathon match  Pros  The gunplay is fun. If Bungie knows how to do anything right, it's gun play. The guns still feel enjoyable to use.   Over all 0/10. Too pricey even if it were free. Bungie made the wrong call here.

Is this really the best Bungie can come up with ? The visuals, SFX and BGM are great (depending on your tastes). I especially love the factions' intro cutscenes.  But as an extraction shooter, it falls short. The pretty visuals become noise that interferes, and the constant beeping of abilities gets in the way of hearing enemy footsteps.  The PvE is serviceable; enemies are a bit tanky, but they hit hard, so it's fair. The PvP, however, is hot garbage, with extremely short TTK. Since this is an extraction shooter, your gear is paramount to your success, except the game is very stingy with standard loot, so you end up running with sponsored free gear and no shield. Not that it matters, since the starting green shield makes no difference in your survivability.  The game keeps throwing free stuff at you with achievements but does not let you test it anywhere, so you're going to lose it very quickly. The game kinda wants you to lose stuff but does not let you easily get new stuff, which is weird.  Actually, one of the worst aspects is how little the game tells you.  Here, you can hack this thing. What does it do ? It summons a wave of AIs. Did it warn me ? Yes, I got an alert after I did the hack and right when they spawned on me. A bit late. Here's an anomalous rift. What does it do ? Dunno; makes you drop your inventory, EMPs you,and probably marks you on everyone's map. What do I get for my trouble ? Beats me, I died before even knowing what I was supposed to do with the thing. Here's a room full of gas; you need to clear it. How ? Why ? Dunno. Don't care; have a bullet at the back of your head while you were thinking. Oh yeah, you forgot this is a PvP game; you ain't supposed to waste time on side quests or confusing stuff in an extraction shooter.  The Marathon IP deserves better than a half-cooked extraction shooter wearing the skin of a solo exploration game.

This is prob going to my one and only long form review on steam. I am writing this review with the understanding that this won't count towards the overall steam review score as I played and reviewed this during the F2P week. I say that because I want to emphasize that this review is as genuine as it gets and I'm not trying to "review bomb". I gave this game a fair shot, 14? hours in 3 days.     I'll start by saying I like the artstyle. Its unique, it's vibrant, it has that wipEout inspiration and honestly there isn't anything like this on the market. That said this has obviously filtered quite a few people already, which I understand and empathize with wholeheartedly. Bungie's gunplay is mostly the same from Destiny. Which is to say its pretty good. If you've played destiny I don't need to explain any further. Those are the only two things I like about this game. There are a lot of problems with this game but I'll highlight the main ones for me:  1. The onboarding experience is almost non-existent. It appears bungie didn't learn their lesson with Destiny 2's Beyond Light. Having a single mission to teach the very basics should not be considered as a good onboarding experience. Generally when you're playing the game you are fight the Environment (pvpvE) and other players (pvPve). When you're new to the game, you are fighting the game itself in addition to the aforementioned variables. This makes for a very stressful and hostile time while you're trying to learn the basics. Especially when the newcomer is trying to form their opinion on the game.  2. PvP is broken if you know how to abuse it. I have very little experience with extraction shooters, however I have played CS and battlefield for over a decade at this point. That made it pretty easy for me to learn the player spawns within a day of playing on Dire Marsh and Outpost. This lead to some pretty funny moments where I was able to spawn rush a lot of people and exfil with A LOT of good loot. Some people might see this as a good thing but frankly when you're spawning in with purples/golds this can get extremely annoying if you're trying to farm loot. Another problem with the PvP system is how hard it funnels people into exfil camping. Majority of players will get punished when they try and play aggressive. This will slowly lead people into a ratty playstyle(which is fine). However, what I'm going to assume will become a problem over time is exfil camping. Since you cannot play this like a casual shooter and be omega aggressive, exfil camping will become a legitimate strategy for netting some good loot. I personally think this playstyle is extremely boring but I'll be lying if I say that it didn't get me some purples.  3. Seasonal Wipes. Yes I get that this is a genre staple. I simply cannot ignore the amount of time investment the faction system and cradle would require every season. It would basically force me to ONLY play marathon during my free time. Which I simply do not want to.    It perturbs me to think what could have been if Marathon was a singleplayer narrative driven game. Bungie's world builders and lore architects are/were industry leading. The setting to me is extremely interesting. Losing contact with a colony ship only to get a reply a century later is an evocative starting point. It simply isn't worth it for me to go through countless readings for a game I cannot get invested in. If they do decide to expand on this world,[b] I'll be somewhere in the heavens, waiting. [/b]

Mr. Gamin' Counselor Here,  Let's be brutally Honest  Marathon is a niche of a niche. It would've been acceptable if it had come out years ago and by an indie developer. Even several months after launch this game still feels like an early access game.  I played the alpha, i played the beta, and now i've played season 2.   This game suffers from trying to be something it's not. A hardcore shooter. It wants to be a challenge but bungie continues to make the game more for everyone losing whatever little identity it really had.  Solo feels like a true hardcore experience as there is no one there to hold your hand and the enemies feel actually threatening. However, when playing with a group of three (the way bungie always intended it) it feels less like a hardcore shooter and more like apex legends cosplaying as arc raiders.   Movement feels great but the Heat system is one of the worst system in the game and truly one of the worst systems in any game. Sure you can unlock upgrades, but if the base game within 30 seconds isn't fun then its just bad game design. You shouldn't have to spend 2 minutes while your heat bar cools down and you are walking at a snails pace. Who cares what upgrades you get if you can't even tolerate playing to that point.   Night dire marsh is honestly a great map, who knew Marathon artistic design is great when you can't see it. Though art design aside, i did enjoy the dark map creating more tension especially for solo's. Even though calling the night version of dire marsh a "new" map is a cheap and scummy way of presenting new content, i still enjoy my experience on the map.   Loot. Is still incredibly boring. Doing all the puzzles, and pve activities and even going after bosses. The loot you get throughout the experience was overwhelmingly boring. So much filter trash. Even tarkov has a bunch of trash but it's easy to identify what is trash versus Marathon which struggles with its design choice of all the trash scattered throughout. But unlike Tarkov where every kill provides a gun, Marathon decides its not always a thing that you get a weapon to drop nor ammo. Which causes it to be a pain to continue playing the game when you are always popping ammo crates only to feel like your experience is less about exciting looting and more like trying to scav constantly for loot so you can play the damn game. When bungie, a company known for its looter shooter destiny, struggles to provide excitement for loot, you know the game is not going to last long.  Overall, nothing really has changed since launch and overall i have no idea how people can play this game for long periods of time when there isn't much really going for this game, and it feels like its still in early access with how limited the content it actually has. Hell even the Cash store barely has anything it, it's almost like the devs didn't have time to finish anything.
