This review might change the longer I play, while I enjoy the free event.   My initial outset,  a fan of the old Marathon and Bungie's historical franchises I wanted to like Marathon since day one of its' announcement. It feels like it's an extension of the lore from 1 but it doesn't, or rather isn't. It isn't a spiritual successor, either. The linear, then somewhat non-linear story from the perspective of memories of electric sheep, of the original titles isn't present here. It's an extraction shooter, which is a fad. I dislike it. The inventories are small, the time to kill on the player in the starting zone feels too punishing. All of the assets here can make a great immersive simulator, but this ain't it.   I sincerely hope Bungie makes something better with the license for this franchise. Or better yet, uses what they've learned and reboots Oni too. I saw what you did with naming the AI assistant that. That doesn't count. You're better than that.  end of day 1; yeah no chief this ain't it. If it's not the AI, which I get it it's clever but also IMMUNE TO MAP HAZARDS it's players, who aren't friendly. You will fight sweaty Xbox teenagers and sweatier playstation tryhards, all of which hate you and don't mind lowering the player count faster.

I had some fun moments but overall the game is extremely punishing and lacks a sensation of overall progression mission over mission. If you're like me and not as good at FPSes as you used to be back in 2014, this is gonna be an extremely rough time. Low quality gear presents an immense disadvantage to higher quality equipment (which, inevitably, everyone but you seems to have), meaning the rich get richer and the casual players get weaker and more frustrated. With death coming so frequently and so quickly, you really have no chance to get better.   Here is my main complaint: I'd like to have a training mode where I can test weapons and try to figure out kill thresholds, but no such mode exists. For a game so polished and slick, this is a pretty inexcusable omission and should have been available day one. Without a training mode, I go into missions totally unaware of what I'm capable of or how much leeway I have when engaging an enemy runner. Inevitably, I will encounter a runner who easily wins the 1v1 and I come away having learned nothing - the skill progression has no chance to get up and moving. More often than not, my death comes at the hands of an invisible enemy or someone with a sniper rifle waiting for a stark white eyesore (myself) to try and sneak towards a POI. Encounters with enemy players are so rare and so fast (you are often killed before you have a chance to react) that you don't take anything away from it.  They do though. All your stuff.  You're extremely discouraged from experimentation also due to the game's confusing keyword system and lack of an in-game dictionary. What is "Signal Jammer"? What does it do? I don't know. What does the "Control Shield" attachment do? Nobody seems to know, not even the community. How much health do the runners have? We can only guess. How much damage do the guns do? What's their effective range? Who knows. How do you get better at a game nobody understands and the developers are not interested in teaching nor affording chances to learn? The game appears to be more interested in creating opportunities for skilled players to kill new players repeatedly, emphasized by the lack of a skill-based matchmaking system. Without SBMM, you inevitably create a singularity of toxicity and skill creep which no casual player can penetrate.  I'd heard the Rook shell (which allows a player to deploy into a match solo in order to try and get materials and so forth) was the way to go for me, but I found it a pointless exercise. You can't undertake missions (further hamstringing progression) and your toolkit is so barren that you stand essentially no chance against an enemy team on the nightmarish blackout dark Dire Marsh map which is your only avenue for play. Once again, the new player is fed into a meatgrinder for the benefit of the day one veterans.  Ultimately I think my takeaway from Marathon is that if you're new to the game and the genre, you probably won't enjoy it. To you, I do not recommend it. Considering Bungie's present financial state, rectifying the extreme disparity between the new and veteran PvP experience should be their absolute top priority even above rumblings of PvE content. The game's basic gameplay loop is a nonstarter without obvious mandatory additions/changes:  - Feature complete and highly configurable Training Mode with simple enemy Runner AIs to practice against - Alternatively, a team deathmatch or arena mode with quick respawns creating an environment where players can hone their dueling skills and become acclimated to their toolkits - The introduction of skill-based matchmaking to ease players into the game and afford them a chance to learn organically from their experiences against similarly matched opponents - The rework or heavy adjustment of invisibility and other effects which allow enemy Runners to instantly kill their targets without risk to themselves (invisible shotguns, snipers etc.)  I came away from the free play period with more negative feelings than positive. There's some good here; the sense of tension and danger makes for exciting, stressful gameplay. The presentation is elite, the gunplay feels better than Destiny 2's ever did. However, the game has a lot of problems centered mostly around how unapproachable it is. If you're not good at it now, chances are you won't get the chance to change that.

This game is at most, okay. As someone who has put some decent hours into Tarkov, this one feels extra bad when you get behind in a wipe.  The good: The gunplay is good, guns feel weighty and seem like they make sense for the universe. Characters are differing and widespread in terms of gameplay options and looks, providing a decent spread of playstyles.  The bad: There is practically no story in this game that is provided. Coming from previous world rich experiences like the original Halo games and Destiny, this feels like a far cry from what I am used to with a Bungie title. If I have to google the story of previous Marathon games that essentially came out 3 decades ago, thats not the kind of story/world building that grabs me. I personally dont enjoy the bland, over explanation in the exposition when you finish a contract/level up a vendor. It feels like they are trying to cram a story down your throat without putting much effort into it. The art style that I loved from Destiny and Halo seems completely gone. Everything, and I mean EVERYTHING in this game to me screams that it was made by AI. The art, characters, and maps feel soulless and lacking a certain polish that I come to expect from Bungie. The color pallet seems bland and uninteresting, and the "Vendors" also seems like they were made up by ChatGPT. From my understanding that's the vibe of the Marathon universe, but it doesnt sit well with me in todays age of an overabundance of AI usage. It makes it feel lazy and AI reliant rather than an artistic choice. Like I had touched on in my opening statements, this game feels unbelievably punishing if you fall slightly behind the core group of players that play this game every day and hours on end. If you arent going into this as a 3-stack, say sayonara to whatever you bring into the raid, as you will get deleted by people with better guns and gear. At least with Tarkov you had a chance to one shot people who were geared if you had the right map knowledge and positioning.  All in all, I cant really recommend this game to anyone who previously enjoyed the Bungie gameplay loops or their art style. It doesnt feel like a Bungie game, but more like an indie title that almost doesnt deserve the price point. Feels like a $20 budget game, and not worth the ~$400Mil it cost to make, nor the $3.5Bil that Sony paid to acquire Bungie.

Game is a complete mess on a technical level. Performance is pretty bad even at lowest settings, and the fact that Bungie laid off basically all of their QA team is apparent at times. The enemy AI is so primitive that you can easily escape and dance around them. They have really basic checks for getting alerted which you can abuse in multiple ways. Stealth is an important part of the game (which I do find fun), but the PvE systems are so stupid and basic that I feel zero pressure or fear from bots unless there is an enemy team around. If it gets too dicey then there's multiple really strong and effective ways to evade. It's also sooo easy to juggle aggro. Many people (who might be stupid) praise the AI for being able to: take cover, flank, retreat when the fights aren't going there way, etc... I don't know how to break to these people that this is the literal bare minimum for PvE AI in the year this game was released. The cops in GTA V do all of that, and that game's ancient. I've played Ubislop stealth games with more complex AI. Compare Marathon to a game like Arc Raiders which pioneered using machine learning techniques to make their enemy AI as interesting to fight as possible... I just expect waay more from a studio like Bungo.  Bungie also clearly doesn't know what they're doing here in terms of game balancing, mechanics, and general systems. Why have wipes if people that played all of last season get purple gear? Why make a hero shooter in the big 26 at all? I don't mind the lack of SBM cause I'm definitely above average skill which means I'm usually stomping people, but no loot based match making is CRAZY in a game like this, and I also don't blame people that want SBM. there's also no point in looting a lot of the time because the pools are so bad, and I can just kill players for their good loot. It makes the "looter shooter" part feel kind of like an after-thought at least in the early levels. Looting the environment should be the focus, and the ARMORY existing in it's current form is dumb. I thought the point was that you were supposed to be worried about losing gear? But, I'm not, cause I can just buy a serviceable loadout in the frankly unlikely event that someone manages to kill me.  The new player experience is also terrible, they have clearly learned 0 lessons from Destiny 2 about the importance of onboarding new players. A game like Apex Legends has 3-4 separate tutorials, this game has 1 and it barely scratches the surface of the game. They also do the classic plunder of making you go to every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ menu in the game after the tutorial, and like come on man, everyone knows you don't do that.  The combat is... fine. It feels EXTREMELY similar to Apex (that'll happen in a hero based shooter with 3 cooldowns, with loot and loot rarity, in a big map with multiple teams), with the exception of the guns, and closer quarter nature of the maps. The gun play is fine, everyone glazes it, but it's just fine imo. Basically every FPS worth playing right now has equal or better gunplay. Battlefield 6 has really good gunplay, but you don't see people talking about it front and center like they do with Marathon because it's a good game ASIDE from how it feels to shoot people. A couple guns feel really good. Like really REALLY good, but most guns feel boring. The bully SMG literally feels like the worst SMG in my Destiny 2 vault that I need to delete because it's never been touched due to how boring and bad it is.  All of this combined with a server outage that lasted nearly the entire first day of the season has shown me that Bungie has not learned anything from Destiny 2. I do not have any faith in them as a company, and it's really obvious that some of the same issues are impacting this game as well despite it being a new codebase.  The final issue (and biggest imo) is the community. This game actually does have items that encourage cooperation among players, but the community insists that being anti-social murder hobos is the way the game was meant to be played. The community is also really toxic, and is slowly becoming a cult. The subreddit (r/marathon) is a great place to view that. I've also noticed that the community really likes to fetishize LGBTQ relationships and sex, specifically lesbians, but also gay men and trans/gender non-conforming people. The whole LGBTQ+ diaspora pretty much. There are also a lot of people that want this to be a gooner game for some reason, and I just think that's repulsive. I also don't think it's a coincidence that I have never seen a single clip of a female content creator that actively plays this game... I wonder why that is? obviously not every game is for everyone, but if an entire demographic (which make up at least 30% of gamers) is that under represented then it does say something. I know 3 other women who are interesting in the game, but 0 of us want to play it, and a lot of that comes from going on reddit and seeing the mostly male player base fetishize lesbians, and beg for gooner skins of the characters. That combined with the culture of toxic sweatiness? yea, that's a no from me dawg, and most other women too evidently  The easiest way to resolve a lot of this is that the devs need to make cooperation necessary to complete certain tasks, this is what Arc Raiders and Seas of Thieves did. In Sea of Thieves they combined really hard PvE encounters that force cooperation with a dedicated PvP faction that makes PvP a little more difficult, but significantly more rewarding. I think Marathon should take notes from that system. They already have a faction that encourages PvP, but they need to do some serious tuning and make the rewards for that faction better, and do other things to make groups team up. Changes like that cool off a lot of the toxic sweaty mindset, and thus make it more appealing to a wider audience. It also means that people can form connections and make friends through the game, which is really important for it's longevity and community building. This won't fix all the problems the community has, but it's the only real starting point at this juncture.  I do like the game's the contract system. It's nothing revolutionary, like not even a little bit, but it is fresh to the extraction shooter genre and I did enjoy it quite a bit. Doing a mission in a map where other people are trying complete their own tasks and kill you does present some interesting challenges, and I did thoroughly enjoy it. The act of completing most contracts is decently engaging and fun, I just wish that it was married to systems that weren't so utterly meh. I would like to play more of this game or a game with a similar system, but the game design choices, history of bad decisions from Bungie, and the community kind of make it radioactive. I also am not going to hold my breath on these things being changed, because currently this game doesn't even have a shooting range and that's a feature that took a DECADE to add to their last game.  Art style is also amazing, probably the the best part of the game for me personally, which is really funny because it's plagiarized lmao.

Least beginner-friendly game I've ever played.   Could've been a lot better. First of all, the menus are poorly designed and hard to navigate. Any desire I had to jump in was quickly dashed as I had to constantly backtrack through the menus. The "Runner" characters aren't nearly fast enough. Just based off the advertising, Anyone would've assumed a more fast-paced style of gameplay but moving around actually feels clunkier than any FPS from the past five or ten years. The CPU enemies are way too powerful, and that wouldn't even be that problematic if the combat weren't so awful. The overall experience is bad enough but the real cherry on this big fat pile of sci-fi guff is the hideous art style and generic universe.   I've played more than enough games to know that space federations usually beginning with the letter "U" (UNSC sounds nearly identical, but that's probably a coincidence) are ubiquitous but pretty uninspired, and super-evil mega corps who own these so-called "Runners" and make them work for profit is also unoriginal. The art style is original, and I respect the concept, but this leaves the map looking like like a 3D-Printed brain aneurysm covered in highlighter fluid which just feels really unprofessional.  I get that Bungie wanted to try something original, but it doesn't seem like it paid off. It had a real tough time scraping together a million copies sold and doesn't feel like it stands out with all the sci-fi buzzwords and overhyped extraction-shooter gameplay. It was also made by two of the most hated studios in the whole industry (Sony and Bungie), which might explain the lower than expected sales. I don't entirely understand apprehension towards Sony (Was it Concord?) but Bungie has made some bad decisions and also shut down their biggest game to keep this mediocre flop alive. In the end, I can't recommend this game to anyone who values their time or money. If you were in the mood for a fun, quirky, and original game, play Helldivers 2. I get that this is an unfair comparison, but you'll get more fun and tons of memorable moments for the same price. Or if you want to join the rank of the many tryhards who love the extraction-shooter genre, this is the tripe you deserve.  Solid 4.5 out of 10.

After a few hours, I can say that Marathon is one of the most frustrating extraction shooters I’ve played.  The gunplay is solid, but almost everything around it feels like a step backward for the genre. The biggest issue is the complete lack of a secure container/safe pocket system. In most successful extraction shooters, players can at least protect a small amount of valuable loot after a death. Here, one bad encounter, disconnect, or desync can wipe out everything you spent the entire raid collecting. Instead of creating tension, it often creates frustration.  The technical state of the game is another major problem. The start of Season 2 was plagued by desync issues, rubber-banding, and server instability. There is nothing enjoyable about losing a fight because what you see on your screen doesn’t match what the server sees. In an extraction shooter where every item matters, poor netcode and unreliable servers are unacceptable.  What makes this even more disappointing is that Marathon was reportedly an extremely expensive project to develop, yet the game launched with technical problems, questionable design decisions, and a lack of features that players expect from the genre. It raises serious concerns about the long-term sustainability of the project and whether the current business model can attract and retain enough players.  At this point, I would not be surprised if the game eventually follows the path of many struggling live-service titles and transitions to a free-to-play model in an attempt to rebuild its player base.  Marathon has good shooting mechanics and an interesting visual style, but those positives are buried under frustrating design choices and ongoing technical issues. Until the servers are consistently reliable, desyncs are addressed, and the game introduces better protection against losing everything due to factors outside the player’s control, I cannot recommend it.  Not Recommended.

Great game, but I think it cost too much to make for what it delivered. I think its a solid extraction shooter with solid ideas, but I feel it missed the nail on a few things: - Abilities kinda make and also ruin the game. Its awesome to rush someone with the destroyer dash, or shoot someone in the face with a grapping hook, but honestly it makes things hard to balance. People start running meta shells like 2 recons so you can always know where everyone is. Contrary to arc/tarkov in which hiding is an actual strategy.  -  nade spamming was never really fixed, instead of limiting the grenade count that you can take into the raid they just change stack sizes and buy amounts. Limiting buy amounts just means you buy them on shop rotation, and might mean people that do not do that end up having to raid without bringing appropriate nades.  - Giving people free stuff early in the wipe especially people that played the last one just ends up rushing the season. End season extraction shooters are always lame and stale, so I dont see why progression would be artificially sped up like that. - Patches seem to take quite a while for properly addressing issues, improper strats like the nade spam or exploitable areas.   All in all, if the budget for the game was lower like 70mil, I think they would've been fine and have gotten less bad press (although with destiny being cancelled and the people brigading, I think it was somewhat expected).

At the time of writing this, I have about 10 hours in Marathon during the free week that was offered. I might put in a little more time before it ends, but overall I have to lean toward not recommending the game in its current state, despite enjoying the gunplay, vibe, atmosphere, and the very interesting world that I would love to learn more about.    Marathon is a game that I want to love because it does so much right in terms of how it feels and plays. At the same time, it sometimes feels so committed to its world that it becomes confusing to navigate. The UI can feel clunky, quests are often vague enough that I spend more time trying to figure out what or where my objective is, and the overall new-player onboarding is extremely unfriendly. It makes it difficult to play with friends unless everyone plans to sweat.    I'm not saying all of the strange, mysterious quirkiness needs to be removed, nor do I think there needs to be a full PvE mode where everyone is 100% friendly to each other. I actually think the current test mode is a step in the right direction for that kind of content. However, I do think the game needs a proper onboarding experience that allows players to learn and grow before being thrown into the depths of hell. The "beginner area" is actually a nice idea, but with the way experience is currently awarded and the lack of meaningful onboarding, you end up flying through it before you've really learned anything or become comfortable.    I enjoy my fair share of PvP experiences and have sunk plenty of hours into games like Overwatch, Apex, Valorant, some CS, and most recently Deadlock. So I am by no means opposed to PvP encounters. In fact, I was excited for Marathon because, while I've also spent time with a game like Arc Raiders (surprise surprise), Marathon seemed to encourage more intense PvP interactions. To be fair, it absolutely delivers on that intensity, just not always in the best way due to some of the issues mentioned above.    Overall, I truly want to see this game survive, but in the current live-service climate, I can't see myself spending money without worrying that the game could be gone by next year. It's an unfortunate feeling because I would genuinely like to support it. At the same time, this is a game backed by Bungie and Sony, two companies that should already have the resources to support it, and yet we've seen how even successful IPs like Destiny 2 have struggled at times.    If Marathon can avoid the EOS plague and continue improving on these issues and more, it could become a great experience. Even if it never becomes one of the most-played games, there should still be room for a game like this to thrive without constant concerns about being shut down. Until then, though, I'll be taking a wait-and-see approach. Unless you're a die-hard extraction shooter fan or a dedicated Bungie fan, I'd suggest doing the same for now.

[h2]The "Hardcore" Illusion[/h2] Is it a good game? Yeah. Would I recommend it? [b]Not really.[/b] Marathon is uniquely frustrating because losing feels completely miserable, unlike other games where you can still enjoy a match even in defeat.   It markets itself as a hardcore extraction shooter, but that "hardcore" label just translates to pure frustration, failed runs, and massive time-wasting.  [h2]Forced Trios & The LFG Nightmare[/h2] The game heavily pushes you into trios. Duos are locked behind a time-based rotation, and I'm obviously not going to wait around just to play with one friend.   This forces you to look for teammates in LFG servers. Spending 10–15 minutes just to find a squad is a total time-waster, and god forbid you lose a run—people will just [u]rage-quit and abandon you[/u]. It happened to me constantly.  [h2]Brutal TTK & Gear Fear[/h2] The Time-to-Kill (TTK) is [b]incredibly low[/b]. Defenders claim a blue or purple shield stops you from instantly dying if an enemy spots you first, but the reality is you rarely have time to react.   The moment a teammate gets downed, a sense of impending doom sets in. You know you're about to lose all the valuable gear you spent hours looting, forcing you back to a basic sponsored kit to start from scratch.  Equipment like grenades is also [b]way too overpowered[/b]. They either leave you on the brink of death through a decent shield or instantly kill you if you have a low one. In Apex Legends, ordnance is useful, but it doesn't entirely decide the fight's outcome.   [h2]Progression: A Daytime Job[/h2] You die—a lot. The game hides behind the excuse that "dying is normal," but there is a limit to a player's patience. It feels incredibly unfair to get sniped from afar while wearing a grey shield by a team kitted out in full purple gear.  Progression is painfully slow. To actually complete contracts, you practically need an [u]unemployed phase[/u] just to grind the game out.  [h2]The Positives[/h2] While the core loop is frustrating, the game does have undeniable highlights:  [b]1. Satisfying Audio Design[/b] With good studio headphones, the soundscape is mesmerizing. The soundtrack is fantastic; I constantly listen to the OST—especially my favorite track, "Stars That Bleed"—on my daily commute to college.  [b]2. Great Gunplay[/b] Every weapon has a distinct purpose and feels great to control. Engaging both AI bots and real players is genuinely fun.  [b]3. Captivating Atmosphere & Graphics[/b] The simplistic, brutalist sci-fi aesthetic is stunning and instantly hyped me up before launch. The atmosphere creates genuine tension, keeping you on your toes the moment you hear distant gunfire.  [h2]Final Verdict[/h2] I’m sticking to [b]Apex Legends[/b]. It feels much more fair, lacks gear fear, and respects your time. Balancing modern 3D shooters with aiming, abilities, and complex maps will always involve a luck factor. If I wanted perfect balance, [u]I'd just stick to chess.[/u]

It's definitely a bit of a funny one. This game's been somewhat controversial for valid and not-so valid reasons, so I've steered away from it up until the free to play week, where I gave it a shot.  Presentation wise, Marathon is an absolute mess. As someone that's used to very poor and messy UIs, the layout of everything still surprised me with how confusing and poorly laid out everything is. It's perfectly fine to try to do your own thing and make something new, but this initial impression is already quite bad - though I powered through it and got on with it.  The actual Art style is another area that's very hit and miss. I, personally, severely dislike it. I find it to be messy, overtly busy and downright ugly. I'm willing to accept that some people enjoy it, but those people also need to be willing to accept that this is an art style that's going to appeal to a very small amount of people. That being said, in gameplay, it works a bit better than you'd expect, as the shapes and colors define themselves well enough, allowing you to (mostly) get a clear picture of everything.  At this point, the game's already made several bad impressions - but when you get into the game? Honestly, the actual gunplay and movement feels very solid. There's a decent feedback for most guns (other than the base rarity ones, which feel extremely piddly, but that's a part of it, at least). The NPCs feel fun enough to shoot, and firefights feel suitably tense. The audio design is a huge boon to it too - you can clearly hear echoing gunfire from quite a bit of distance, which lets you more or less pin-point where combat is taking place, and the VAing is really nice. For a while, I definitely thought my initial impressions of the game were slightly too negative - but in all honesty, this did not last.  Firstly, finding games took a shockingly long amount of time. 10 to 15 minutes waiting overall - for a game that's on a free to play week, that's really rather poor, and not very promising. Secondly, I ran into connectivity issues multiple times - considerable lag spikes that kept dragging my character back mid-spike, or replaying already done reload animations - making things feel floaty and awkward when they really shouldn't, given the actual movement is so crisp.  My impressions of the Player Versus Player component of the game were quite poor, as all I've really ran into were people corner camping even with full teams. While I understand that's part and parcel for extraction shooters, it definitely feels quite off when combined with this movement system.  Overall, I honestly can see the glimpse of something good in Marathon, but it completely failed to pull me in, and justify itself as an Extraction Shooter - which was the whole purpose of the free to play week. The actual technical issues were baffling (though the game at least does seem well-optimized), and the new player experience is straight up bad, made even worse by an inexplicably poorly laid out UI.  Some people will enjoy this for what it is, and that's fine. But when asking if I recommend it or not, I honestly don't. If Marathon interests you, you probably already got it. But if you're curious about the remaining time on the F2P week, I really can't say the game sold itself to me at all.

The game suffers from several poor game design decisions.  When you spawn into a squad-filled trio, each player often has completely different contracts and objectives. Instead of encouraging cooperation, this incentivizes players to force their own progression on their teammates. Everyone is effectively playing for themselves while sharing the same lobby.  Even with the addition of a duo queue, it remains more efficient to queue as a trio. You can benefit from a third teammate's contract progress and then simply leave them behind once their objective has been completed, since the extraction of all three squad members is not required to receive experience and progression rewards. This creates incentives that actively work against teamwork.  The wipe system is also frustrating. Being forced to repeat the exact same contracts over and over after every wipe quickly becomes tedious rather than engaging. Instead of creating a sense of progression, it often feels like you're stuck on a treadmill.  The introduction of the Cradle, which allows players to allocate skill points and customize their Runner, is particularly ironic because it was one of the most heavily requested features before release. However, it also directly contradicts the game's original Hero Shooter direction. If character customization and build crafting are so important that they had to be added later, it raises questions about whether the Hero Shooter framework was the right choice in the first place.  The game may excel in its art direction, technical execution, and visual presentation, but the core gameplay loop is genuinely awful. The progression system is simply not fun to engage with, and it constantly feels like it is wasting the player's time rather than rewarding it.  In solo play, the experience is reduced almost entirely to “solo vs solo” encounters where stealth and information matter more than gun skill. As a result, the Assassin-style Runner with invisibility becomes the dominant meta, since discretion and sound discipline effectively decide fights. If you are not playing into stealth and invisibility, you are at a constant disadvantage, which severely limits viable playstyles and reduces overall variety.  Marathon is a game with incredible visuals and production value, but beneath that impressive presentation lies a fundamentally flawed gameplay and progression structure that repeatedly undermines player enjoyment.

TLDR; this is a beautiful game, but the onboarding and matchmaking is horrendous.  Tried it during the Season 2 free play event and got very lucky in that I was able to complete the first few contracts in Perimeter Beginner without too much trouble, but as soon as I got into the real servers, I was able to complete a single contract during my five or so hours due to being killed within the first couple minutes despite crouching and being extremely careful the entire time. It does not help that the matchmaking takes as long as it does, meaning I was waiting for matches much longer than I was actually playing. This might be a skill issue and if you don't mind spending dozens of hours to learn the game before being able to have fun, this game might be for you.   The UI is very confusing with inconsistent controls and most item icons are identical, making them very difficult to distinguish without reading the, for the most part, confusingly written descriptions.   I was really hoping to like this game, as I adore the visual design, but the matchmaking is simply frustrating. I would gladly try the game again in the future if Bungie improves the SBMM to give new players a chance, for example taking the previous season's rank in to account at the beginning of a new one.

This will be a lot so TLDR: Buy it on a BIG sale only! There's a lot to complain about so just know I like this game and all of these comments are me wanting it to get better.  Its not bad but its definitely valued at free to play.  There are too many issues and unrefined parts of the game to not call it a Beta.   The Shells/Characters are well defined and while some are more appealing because of game mechanics like healing being a limited resource (triage) they're all seemingly very useful. The thief is also incredible being that this is a loot game. Rook is interesting but not being able to do contracts has kept me away from trying it.  The cradle is a really well done long term list of upgrades for your shells and its nice having made meaningful progression even when you die and lose a run.  In ♥♥♥♥♥♥ Bungie fashion duos is a limited mode for some reason? The mode doesn't seem to leave but at the same time you can don't get to play duos on any map besides the one the game chooses that day. I cant imagine making that poor of a decision for a mode.  The heat/stamina system is a cool idea because of its interaction with things like incendiary grenades and rain and sitting water but its overall just bad. You'll get to upgrade your heat capacity slowly over time but you will find yourself standing still or walking slowly out in between areas just too often. It hurts the enjoyment of the game more than it adds to it. I don't mind certain actions like sliding having a great cost but just regular sprinting causing you to have to stop/walk for 35% of a match is too much.  The time to kill is actually well balanced although snipers can easily end your run instantly and they're also really easy to acquire so maybe the damage at range can be tuned to make them less oppressive but given their accessibility it doesn't feel unfair.  The inventory space it just bad. If you're going in with gear and a specific quest or objective you want to do its perfectly fine. The problem is when you start playing you need to pick up so many things for your Faction upgrades and you will never have enough space. A too large portion of your default inventory is taken by ammunition because the AI in the game are just brain dead bullet sponges. You can literally peak a corner/wall and they don't know how to shoot you but fortunately for them if your hitting all headshots it will still take an average of 10-15 rounds depending on the damage of your gun of course just to kill the weakest enemies. (Excluding ticks that die to the wind) [After playing more solo, ammunition is such a glaring problem! Bot health needs to be reduced significantly. I cant be expected to use 5 to 6 inventory slots for ammo just to do 2 crate events while  hitting 90% headshots on bots that have nothing in their head to compensate for their ridiculous health pools.] The only way around this awful design is to abuse invisibility so you can loot the crates with no effort instead of spending 3-4 stacks of ammo and a few minutes of your very limited time.]  The clarity of what you do with most of the junk you see while looting is fine because it tells you what its for but there's so much crap when your starting out you'll feel like you're reading more than playing since you don't have the space to keep everything.  I haven't played all the characters yet but a couple of issues with Triage is activating your 3rd ability causes you to stand up for no reason even though the ability doesn't restrict you from going right back to crouched so if your behind cover keep that in mind.  Speaking of cover you can't prone in this game which isn't a bad thing at all except for a few areas (most notably railings with metal sheets as cover) where even when you're crouched you can still get shot in the head while you cant see over the cover you're crouched behind. I will say this is very rare of an issue but it should be fixed.  Triages heal drone is generally very good at tracking team members but sometimes won't and because you throw it and it bounces on a rare occasion it will get stuck somewhere and be unattainable with no way to recall it.  The UI is one of the worst I've ever come across and the amount of messy menuing you're subjected to is crazy.  Clicking on attachments sometimes doesn't auto equip them and the same with bags. There's genuinely so many small annoyances with menuing I cant be bothered to list them. The UI for the store is laughably bad. When you want to salvage multiple items in your vault UI be ready for when each time you add an item to the list you get sent back to the top if your vault. -_- Automatically leaving the loot UI when taking damage should obviously be in an extraction shooter and for some reason its just not here? You cant even move a little while looting so one or the other needs to be added if not both.  The contracts/quests you get are generally well explained enough for you to go to the correct location but you'll have to roam around a bit before getting an actual marker telling you where to go. Other contracts are even less telling which isn't too bad but just more annoying than the system has to be at all. Being that this is an extraction shooter you are going to get shot in the back while you try to do your quests very often so it'd be nice if you didn't have to waste time roaming until you find exactly what you're looking for to get a meh amount of rank up XP. The Factions that you rank up are cool but the rewards are very lackluster. I need to get a Faction to rank 10 just to gain the ability to ***purchase*** a ♥♥♥♥ weapon in the armory... There's plenty that is worth unlocking but at least 70% of it is slop. I don't want to grind a Faction so I can buy ♥♥♥♥♥♥ green/blue attachments. All the purchasable items are also just a way of recuperating your build after you die which by the time you've ranked these Factions up you'll most likely have quite a full vault of better gear than the crap they let you purchase until the very last upgrades that offer purple items. [Extra small nitpick: Please stop giving me a mini cutscene every time I turn in a contract. I don't care.]  The codex is full of garbage bloat.  There's mid rewards mixed in with useless entries that only serve as a notification for me to get rid of.  The battle pass is nicely done and they don't disappear with each new season but I do think like this current seasons exclusive knife skin there will always be a cosmetic that you can only get by purchasing the BP during that season.  If you want a good quality Extraction Shooter you should absolutely give this one a go at at least 50% off or honestly when it almost inevitably becomes free to play. I cant stress enough that I want people to buy this game as it improves but it is not a $40 game. For what's actually a beta, that's a joke.

It feels exactly like they wanted to make a game with all of the popular trends "Extraction Shooter,Abilities/Heroes" and fast paced while make the game like how bungie traditionally would. This ends up feeling like there is a significant gap in design where the puzzle pieces just don't fit together as it doesn't seem like they understand what makes a "Extraction Shooter" fun and what the critical flaws of that genre is even or at least they choose to ignore those flaws.  The design in general for audio/graphics and so forth are all pretty good even impressive (Ignoring the guns) but the important part gameplay wise feels exactly like a knock off spin of tarkov and some other games while lacking a critical flow/balance that is enjoyable. I don't find it enjoyable to wait 5-10 minutes in queue just to queue up again and die within 10 minutes just because they wanted to make the game faster pace without realizing that half of the time will be spent in the lobby which is arguably even worse than tarkov.  1. The maps are small which when combined with abilities makes it incredibly easy to get pounced on while trying to do any objective. Style over substance.  A classic Macintosh game rehashed into sweaty competitive console slop, and it still costs money. I don't like extraction shooters, but this one doesn't even have a useful inventory GUI. You will encounter a massive skill gap early on playing the game in the PvP. No easy keyboard shortcuts for sorting the loadout Vault or saving custom buying presets for the Armory gear shop. Sorting inventory is hell. The maps are just shoebox sized arenas between tall obstacles. Maps all play predictably, you spawn on the outside ring and move further inwards in coincentric circles of loot, spiraling in the same fashion on each map. Whoever reacts first wins, I'm too old for that ♥♥♥♥. Most fights are boring corridor shooting or hiding behind crates. Minimal opportunity for fighting through complex terrain in enfilade/defilade, or hiding in foliage. 'Press E to hack the computer network' nonsense. Weapon progression and weapon archetype balancing means you start with near useless peashooters and your enemies can pick you off at their pleasure with more effective gear. Contracts are just fetch quests. Not being able to queue for multiple gamemodes at the same time means waiting forever (unless you are in North America). No real dynamics for CQB melees beyond three types of canned animations. Progession unlocks that provide strong advantage stack the odds against from new players. Open mic VOIP enabled by default. No Linux build because the intended culture of the game encouraged cheaters to fester before it launched. Entirely matchmaking bound - No custom servers, server browser, or dedicated server package for third party hosts. No source development kit for modding or mod hosting integration. At least it looks and sounds somewhat unique. Just play the tutorial during the free weekend. There will be recurring free weekends to bring fresh targets for the regulars to shoot at.

I can't say I've played much of this game but I'll start with the good:    The atmosphere of the game seems pretty cool, very System Shock-esque (until you load into a round, then it just becomes a normal shooter with a theme on it)    There's some nice interactivity with the scenery being able to break vents and close window shutters.    You get a bunch of characters to play as immediately.    Unfortunately for the bad however:    TTK seems pretty low, which for games with quick respawns like Death Matches and Team Death Matches is fine because if someone gets the drops on you and kills you in under a second then no worries, you just respawn and go again, however when you've just spent 10 minutes picking out a loadout, finding a server, and loading into a map then 10 minutes looting it's not a great feeling to be downed before you can even fire off 5 bullets.    The TTK to finish a downed player is also super low. Most of the time playing Duos my teammate (or I) will get finished about 2 seconds after getting downed meaning that even if you do win the overall fight, your teammate will have to just sit on their hands whilst you finish the mission.    There's no feedback on how much damage you're doing during or even after a fight. You get told how much damage you took from various sources when you die but have no idea how close the fight was. Most fights turn into us each holding down the trigger point blank until one of us falls down, but I have no idea if I nearly got the guy or not.    A lot of the guns feel very indistinctive, they all have the same blocky design and the sounds don't particularly stand out either, it's also not particularly clear what attachments can go on a gun and can't. (my assault rifle can't take optics but the pistol can?)  There's also no where to test out different guns to see what you 'click' with. (also could be a skill issue but certain guns really feel like you're just playing airsoft with how little damage they do).    match making also feels pretty rough, seems my first games were getting wiped by fully kitted guys who knew what they were doing rather than being in beginner lobbies.    This game doesn't feel very intuitive, took a lot of just poking around to find what does what.    Also not sure why the maps are "Solo or Trios". I would like to do duos with my friend but if I try that then I either have to auto fill the remaining slot and have some random guy tagging along or have to do 2 vs 3 fights... weird choice.    Not being able to take the small healing items you got during the mission out with you feels like a weird choice as well... Why do I have to find them in round every time?    Overall, if you want an extraction shooter, go play Arc Raiders, they just do it better.

Let me start by stating that I received my copy of Marathon via its open play week.  At the time of this review, I have put about six hours into the game. It has a lot of potential, but I do not believe it is at a level that justifies paying for it in its current state.  I’ll begin with the positives:  1.) The overall visuals, and the detail in the codex entries for events and units are well done.  2.) The corporate satire is on point, especially the system “cheering” you on after you start a losing streak.  3.) The story delivered through contracts seems interesting and has room to grow.  4.) Losing all of your gear upon death forces you to play more conservatively in both PvP and PvE, which fits the extraction‑shooter idea, even if I do not always enjoy it.  My main complaints are:  1.) UI  One of the biggest issues with this game is locating contract objectives. The contract will tell you the general location (for example, Northern and/or Southern Relay) but gives you almost nothing to work with beyond that. If you do not have a party member who already knows the layout, you end up spending most of your time running through the entire area trying to find a single terminal. The terminal blends into the background and only gets an interaction prompt or location icon when you are very close to it. If PvP were optional, I would not mind this as much; it would feel like old Morrowind‑style “read the directions and explore.” But with other players hunting you, it just becomes frustrating busywork instead of tension.  2.) Contracts to progress the story  On paper, tying story progression to contracts is a good idea. In practice, it is not enjoyable when you mix other players and vague UI into it. You spend a good chunk of time just running across the map to reach the general area, then a couple more minutes combing around for the objective while dealing with NPCs and players, only to get killed and have to start over. When that loop takes four, six, or eight matches to finally complete one contract, the novelty wears off very quickly.  3.) Losing all of your equipment upon failing the run  For PvP, I understand the intent: it encourages more careful, tactical play instead of everyone sprinting around like Call of Duty. The problem is that it makes investing in higher‑tier weapons, armor, and mods feel pointless. If you queue Trios and get matched with a bad team or make one big mistake, all of your effort and gear are gone. After the first few games of getting stomped by higher‑ranked players, I defaulted to the free starter kit because there was no reason to risk anything better.  I also do not enjoy spending most of a match looting, only to lose everything at the end and receive a tiny amount of XP toward a seasonal rank that will be wiped after a few months. There is some long‑term progression through factions and the Armory, but moment to moment it still feels like “bring gear in, lose gear, slowly crawl a bar forward.” I do not feel motivated to invest money or time into a system where the main reward is essentially, “good job, here is a season level that will be reset.”  You can argue about player skill in the PvP all you want, but a lot of successful play boils down to being opportunistic and third‑partying people with high‑damage or high‑DPS weapons. That is part of the genre, but combined with full gear loss and weak onboarding, it makes the early experience miserable.  If there is one thing that could be fixed to make the game significantly more enjoyable, it would be the UI. Even adding a general marker or search radius on the HUD for the contract objective would improve the experience dramatically. Making waypoint markers easier to see on the compass and visually differentiating objective terminals from the environment would also help a lot. In addition, adding either a dedicated “hardcore” mode for full gear loss or a separate, more PvE‑focused story campaign would give players more options for how they want to engage with the game’s systems.  TL;DR: Marathon has promise, but the onboarding, contract UI, seasonal wipes, and harsh gear loss make it hard to recommend right now.

Small visual indicators about your characters movement like gun sway give great feedback to the player. I actually liked the blocky, grid based UI design. Its simple and modern. The character abilities are cool and unique.   Cons: The environment has plenty of details like switches, panels, tablets, etc. that are brightly colored compared to their immediate surroundings and look like they should be interactable. I was often met with frustration trying to interact with things that are just static objects in the game world.   Game worlds felt really vast and devoid of other players. The brief interactions with other players ended fast and then I was left wandering a vast world feeling anxious I would get attacked. The game desperately needs new play modes that either focus entirely on PVE or condense players into more constant PVP.

[h3]You can release the best game ever made in existence, but if you've already burnt a huge chunk of your existing fanbase, then you're betting your business on new fans. Interesting strategy, Bungie.[/h3]  When Marathon was announced, Destiny PvP was in a miserable state, while being the most expensive it had even been to play Destiny 2.  The original controls for Halo CE cannot be understated. Bungie made the first controller FPS that felt good. People laughed at the time, but the entire console shooter genre was build upon this.  Destiny 1 had the weirdest UI decision at the time. Also, different aim assist stats for each weapon. People laughed at the time, but i still think it was revolutionary to have an FPS game where the player can choose a weapon based on how easy it is to land shots with it. Also assassin's creed and CoD started copying this menu despite having no reason for it.  Somewhere along the development of Destiny 2, Bungie had some kind of stroke. They decided that M+KB was the best way to play Destiny 2. Not that i have a problem with players choosing M+KB, but Bungie was my go to Controller FPS dealer. Yet they introduced REDUCED recoil to mouse, which is the opposite of what Overwatch and Battlefield 6 did, which is reduced recoil for controller players.  Sure Destiny 2 was still playable with a controller, but do you really want to play vs a guy with twice the aim speed and half the recoil? And that's before all the updates that broke PvP.  Enter Marathon. Destiny's iconic ♥♥♥♥♥♥ menu, but this time you use the D-pad AFTER using the Left thumbstick to select the option, so you have to keep switching your left thumb from stick to d-pad. ARE WE BEING SO FOR REAL RIGHT NOW? The studio that CREATED the controller fps can't even do a F00king controller menu? This is like that time when Diablo 4 devs were using a controller to play their click-to-move game.  Sure, maybe im the minority with Repetitive Wrist Strain injury, but i'm not going to cough up money for a game that clearly wasn't built for me, let alone give it a positive review.

Great Concept. But absolutely awful execution.   The PVE enemies are absolutely mind blowing sometimes, with the way they move or react to players actions seeming almost life like at certain points, but then they will just trickle slowly into a room to be killed off one by one instead of charging in with their strength in numbers.   The guns look really nice and account progression seems really thought out, but then you get in a fight with another squad and it comes down to whoever has better gear no matter what. It seems like the developers fell into the pitfall that is weapon/gear rarities, where you just get the same ♥♥♥♥ from the start, but just better, with no thought to how that affects PVP. Dark and Darker is an amazing example of how to deal with this, with their high roller matches, but when it comes to a PVP title where ranged weapons are the standard and can be part of your loadout from the start of the match, weapon rarities instead act like a self made insurance policy instead of something to be risked. Its the reason that early wipe Tarkov is so enjoyed by many, where as the later into the wipe it is, the more mundane or boring it becomes for most players.  A game like this in my opinion could be fixed with some changes, but for now, it is better to stay with games that you already know you enjoy like Arc Raiders or Dark and Darker, if you like an item rarity system that doesn't trump strategy or skill.

I did not enjoy this game. I'll start by saying that I do not have experience with their Destiny games, however I was a big Halo fan since the first one and over time lost interest.  All this to say is that I approached this game without those biases that others might have because this is taking the place of future/current Destiny games or whatever.   Since it was a free week I gave this a spin. Downloaded it on day one and I couldn't get in, kept getting errors that bungie servers were down. I followed the provided bungie help link and totally unhelpful - it didn't even acknowledge that the servers were down so I got the impression that the company doesn't really communicate problems well or have the player in mind. After waiting several hours I was finally able to get on late at night and all went well.   My experiences with extraction shooters are Tarkov, Delta Force and Arc Raiders with all those being pretty fun for me in their different sort of ways. I gave this a go and honestly the graphics are much better in person than they look from screenshots, the audio is top notch and overall immersive, however that's where the positive ends for me.   I did the tutorial in the beginning which was ok, however after playing the actual game I felt the tutorial did not do a good job of actually showing me how to play the game and how to properly use the interface.  Actually that is where I had the biggest problem.    This game was confusing and overwhelming.  I never felt like I knew what I was doing or what was going on, perhaps I'm dumb in this regard but this game highlighted it even more!  I ran around confused and shooting at random things and not really knowing what the goal was or how to achieve it. I was never able to successfully extract; I've got 5 hours in and still have not had a successful extraction.  There's talk of Rooks, runners, sponsored kits, factions etc. and I didn't quite understand what all that meant, it seemed like I was in a complex world in this game and not given the memo on how to operate.  The tutorial did not "memorably" teach me, otherwise I would've known how the systems in this game work.  I've played other extraction games with many hours in and didn't have that problem but this game seemed too confusing and too complicated for me, I just didn't "get it" and the tutorial did not properly teach me how to play the game so perhaps they can improve that, for the casual player.  After 5 hours of playing here and there throughout several days I finally gave up and went to my other games.  I think this is a game you have to grind to "get it" and perhaps then it will be great, kinda like a slow starting movie but like those I lost interest before I got there and I moved on.   I did not like the characters, while the graphics were good I didn't like how things looked, it just had an odd style to it that didn't click with me.  Gameplay definitely seemed smooth but I didn't care for the character design which is a big deal if you want to play as someone, how they look matters to me; I just wasn't on board.  Perhaps if the game felt more accessible, I could overlook the characters but it didn't either. So there was really no reason for me to keep enduring.  If you are looking for something fun, that you can casually pick up and play, this is not it, for those people I do not recommend this game.  However, if you are willing to put the time into it, (definitely more than 5 hours  for me) in order to start enjoying it then maybe give this game a chance.    Overall, I am glad I got to try this game, however I do not ever plan on buying this game, it was not interesting enough for me to hang around and did not motivate me to want to grind further - a hard pass.

[h1]Technical Performance[/h1] Running this absolute optimization nightmare on an RTX 3090 at a measly 1080p and having the audacity to drop into the 30-40 FPS range even with DLSS turned on and settings gutted is a borderline criminal offense.  [h1]User Experience & Onboarding: Hostile and Brain-Dead[/h1] The onboarding experience is a masterclass in how not to design a video game. It mindlessly hurls you into a match with zero context, forcing you to navigate a labyrinth of bloated, pretentious menus that arrogantly assume you have been sweating in the Alpha since day one.  The entire gameplay loop is completely contradictory:  [list] [*] [b]Pointless Loadouts:[/b] Why even bother choosing a loadout when the game lazily showers you with random guns every two seconds? [*] [b]Abysmal PvPvE Design:[/b] You are dropped into a sweaty, hostile PvPvE zone, yet the game expects you to stand still and read essays of text just to figure out what an item does. [*] [b]The "Loot" Illusion:[/b] Half the garbage you pick up is just meaningless vendor trash only worth credits. While the gatekeeping, seasoned players might tolerate this disrespect of their time, for a new player, it is an incredibly frustrating, confusing, and alienating experience. [/list]  [h1]Art Direction & UI: Cheap, Plastic Garbage[/h1] The visual design is an eyesore. The UI is a cluttered, illegible mess that fails at its most basic job: conveying information. Bungie tries to hide behind this pretentious lore excuse that in-universe corporations designed these weapons, but let us call it what it actually is: lazy, uninspired geometry. Every single gun looks like a smoothed-out, generic piece of cheap Mega Bloks knockoff plastic and not in a nostalgic way, but in a "we ran out of budget and talent" way.  [b]TL;DR[/b]: This game is a bloated, unoptimized, pretentious trainwreck. Looking at the catastrophic state of this absolute dumpster fire, it is painfully obvious why Sony has Bungie on the chopping block. Avoid this disaster at all costs.

Marathon is not worth your time and money, it just isn't.  This is a game with effort and budget put into it, I can see where (some) of the reported $250mil budget went without a dobut. But its choice of aesthetic, well polished and intentional though it may be, is incredibly bland feeling in practice despite the vibrant colors. Habitat layouts and elements repeat and while there are of course unique "central feature" elements to each map, the rest is easy to get lost in visually as the samey-ness overwhelms your senses.   Inventory management is unintuitive from both UI and UX perspectives and there's too much "garbage loot" (auto-sold on extract for money) relative to default (even a lightly expanded) in-round inventory. Spending the first hours of the game working out what's garbage, what looks like garbage but can provide rep, and what's needed for advancement is not skill expression. As a default part of the genre experience it serves to do nothing but slow new players down and make them easy-pickings. Other extraction-shooters streamline the "choice paralysis" phase, balancing the loot pool so that there's more "truly useful" than "half-garbage" laying about. Bungie seems to think that garbage-loot's secondary role is providing lore and to that end there's garbage everywhere. The game is also bad about identifying what mods go with which weapons or highlighting which slot the body-mod you just equipped went into. Inventory items (especially in-game consumables like bullets and meds) have low stack sizes for an extraction shooter as well; I understand the balance/gameplay-tension Bungie wanted here but the end result is more annoying that strategic.  Returning to UI/UX, the format and experience is so poor that there's a "help I don't understand anything on this page" button for every screen. Take that in for a second. Moving between inventory, factions, store etc. is a tabbed experience (your standard Press A or D / Press LB or RB), it's fine. Pulling back to the main menu to access your shells and shell-upgrades is not. Each screen just does a poor job of displaying/formatting information for the player; it's not some indecipherable mess, just consistently unintuitive. This lack of "smoothness" in the setup/menu phase extends even to elements like default key-bindings - you can get used to them with time but somehow elements like stack splitting or dropping items are unintuitively implemented.  While very subjective, I think the presence of classes in Marathon adds nothing to the experience but frustration. The class system is so obviously designed around trio-squad gameplay that it's frankly laughable they included a Solo mode. Classes exist, in my opinion, to force players into teamwork more than provide a "deep strategic layer." Each of the classes' powers/abilities would be better served as loadout elements (Marathon already adds slots to player equipment for gameplay-effecting mods) or in-game pickups. In Solo play there are winning classes and there are losing ones, especially as you pump more upgrade points into the meta-progression system,  This leads to the next frustration: a somehow simultaneously underwhelming and over-abundant number of systems to manage. Some clearly inspired by Bungie's work on Destiny 2 in that they exist to provide grindable busywork to keep concurrents up. Even collecting/managing skins becomes tedium when it is mediated by the UI/UX of Marathon, making choices like providing no clear-all button or "forgetting" any organization preference (show only obtained items etc.) combined with how items are presented/organized.  Skins bring me to my penultimate note: weapon design. I understand Bungie chose a specific aesthetic early and decided to stick with it the whole way, I respect the choice but criticize the outcome. Never have I played a supposed AAA FPS with uglier weapons. Pistols, rifles and railguns that are just assemblages of differently-sized voxels with minimal greebling and a matte finish. They look underwhelming in a match, in the inventory/inspect screen they look downright lazy (though of course effort was involved).  Finally, guns bring us to gunplay. Here one has to acknowledge that Bungie has perfected a certain kind of shooter "feel" and Marathon continues that tradition. While NPC enemies are bullet sponges and the Player made of tissue, pulling the trigger on most weapons feels great. The problem is that "base" TTK is so low that even a handful of enemies (who all have ESP once you've been seen) can overwhelm you quickly while the "spongy" nature of many NPCs undermines the fun of shooting. In PvP players are of course caught in the classic "strong prey on the weak" dynamic: you are meat to anyone more kitted than you, though all players go down relatively quick, and (especially in Solos) you will consistently encounter geared campers ready to shut your run down the second you make a noise.  I think the pull is supposed to be the gunplay and the lore. But if you don't care about the lore from the outset (and why should you) there's not enough here to support the "classic Bungie shooting experience."  The only people I can recommend this to are fanatics for Bungie FPS combat and Marathon lore nerds. I'm not even sure I could recommend Marathon if it was F2P based on the time commitment required for any extraction shooter. I certainly can't for $40-50 local currency.

Played during the free week, very glad I did not pay for it when it released. Now, cavate, I am playing solo so maybe it’s better with squads, but it is clear that they put zero thought into balancing the experience for singles.  The way a match currently works if you are solo is you pick one of the faction missions, load into the appropriate map with your free loadout and attempt to decipher the quest objective (ie “Scan four support vehicles” which just means wander around the map until you find four locations with vehicles, but there is no rhyme or reason as to which locations with vehicles count, or “Find the nearby stacked crate” which apparently means look around for two white crates stacked on top of eachother in an area filled with random crates, go up to the qr code printed on it and hold e). If you are lucky, you will not see anyone the entire match and be able to exfil with your meager loot. If you are not, some other player with better gear will see you and kill you, in which case your entire run (5-20 minutes) was wasted and you get nothing (unless you managed to complete the quest objective first which for some reason doesn’t actually care if you successfully extract). There is no culture of "friendlies" here, TTK is short enough that whoever engages first often wins so if you find another player its kill or be killed. The one player player I found who was not outright agressive killed me the second I turned my back and started looting.   Making progress and getting a better loadout is basically impossible. Loot is rare, and by default you can only start with 16 inventory slots to hold all your ammo, supplies and loot. For reference, a pistol takes 4 slots and a rifle takes 8. There are backpacks that increase this capacity, but they are so rare I have never actually found one while looting. And of course, if you want to use a backpack from the shop, you are unable to use the free starting gear and must provide your own. So, you end up in this desperate loop of trying to extract a decent gun with your free kit only to build up a basic loadout and instantly loose everything the next time you die.  Now this isn’t how the game is supposed to work. If you are playing with friends a knock down isn’t an instant death. Your teammates can revive you, or at the very least benefit from your loot if you do die. But there is no rebalancing for solo matches. They didn’t even bother to adjust the number of hostile NPCs with solo matches, so you are left trying to clear entire buildings of hostile NPCs by yourself.   If this game was only pvp or only pve it would be so much better. As it stands though it’s a boring slog with uninteresting gameplay.

The design language introduces a ton of visual noise, the UI/UX is horrendous (the codex being a prime example of menus buried under menus), it's impossible to tell what items are aside from heals/shields/consumable because they're all neon legos, and the pacing is painful slow for the TTK that deletes any per run kitting and progress you may have made. It's prohibitively expensive to run kits, and I can't say that I even had 1 evenly matched squad v squad battle.    On top of this the community is just atrocious, between sweats, grognards trying to get attention screaming that the game is "woke", and the aggressively anti-coop mentality of group compared to other contemporaries like Arc on top of a game that is not a ton of fun to play

I tried on both free weekends, but I find the game just isn't fun. Sorry. Not for me. I realize this is a personal opinion but I find the experience to be frustrating.    -Confusing menus  -Extremely fast dying when attacked -Repetitive levels full of copy-pasted elements  The experience of this game is that you are going around the same levels over and over. You are finding things, trying to navigate the confusing menus (never enough space in your backpack, etc) and then...ZAP you are killed.  Your experience upon dying is usually just to say "huh? I didn't even see that guy."  You don't even have time to plan a counter-attack and have a battle; you're just dead the first time someone shoots you.  Then when you die, it's a long 30 seconds to a full minute of waiting for another chance to play as you go through menus, menus, menus. It just kills the momentum.   Sorry, I really did not like this one to be honest!   For context, I far prefer Risk of Rain 2. That game is surprising and delightful and fun and exciting with a similar concept ("one life only, you'll lose your stuff if you die"). Plus in Risk of Rain when you pick up items they elevate your game in surprising and unpredictable ways.   Meanwhile in Marathon you might be spending time in a crafting menu adding modules that just slightly improve your guns and then you're just killed while you're in the menu and lose everything.   I gave this a chance but this one is just not fun to me.

This review is based of playing it during the Free Weekend.  I really wanted to like this game. The graphics and sound effects are well made. The atmosphere and lore is interesting. Yet the gameplay left things to be desired. I went in expecting it to be this amazing heist game where you would scramble to get to the finish with the loot. Yet most of my experience resulted in either dying too early or getting to the end without a scratch. Usually the PvE aspect felt too easy if you coordinate your team well, yet the PvP aspect is unfair at its nature and could be quite difficult depending on what kind of opponent you meet. I didn't get a rush from exfiltrating nor from winning against enemy players in gunfights. I didn't feel sad or angry when dying, because I already expected everything in my inventory to dissapear as soon as I queue up.  By the time I wrinte this, I've finished roughly 11 hours. I truly wanted to like this game and hope it goes well for it, yet from my current experience, I can't recommend it due to my experiences.  If you're as interested in this game as I was, I'd recommend reading some reviews and if you still aare interested, I'd first hand recommend buying at a discounted price, then decide for yourself if it's worth playing or not.

Using the moth metaphor, bungie needs to remake itself. It has risen time and time again to make great games, the halo franchise, Destiny.... but marathon? For a game that has allegedly been on their radar since the halo days... this feels exactly like just another resource grab game with a whole new lexicon of buffs and materials to figure out. On top of simple figuring out what the hell biomass does, i need to relearn the competitive FPS skillset and there is really no way to gently learn that other than a whole lotta suffering and lost loot. Loot that has little to no wow factor.   the crazy fast TTK is fine but the fact that I'm stuck in menus and waiting for games more than actually playing makes me wonder what I'm even doing here. I just cant see how this game is going to come out on top.  What Bungie ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nails every single time though is aesthetics -- the menus, the lore, the color grading, the costumes hell even the sounds are phenomenal.   That brings me back to the beginning. They need to go back into the cocoon and come out different. These people could be making movies, sagas, television series. the games aren't for everyone but the lore and the art are far too attractive to be only for the sweaty dudes who play nothing else but FPS.  "Escape will make me God" You said it bungie. escape and become something greater than you were. don't just fizzle into the background.

The one thing I like was the atmosphere, that's it. Gunplay and abilities are okay. Your stamina runs out so fast and the ammo economy is garbage. You mag dump into tanky robots like its a GM nightfall with only primary weapons. The robots do way too much damage if they hit you just for you to basically be out of ammo after they are dead and then you have a actual player come in and kill you in a few seconds. You can wait for someone else to shoot the robots and then go kill them. That is what most of this game is. In the couple of 1v1's I had where there was no enemies and the other player and I both had full health, I was able to kill them easily but actually getting straight 1v1's with no interference is really difficult. I could not find players the way I wanted to. Maybe I am bad at extraction shooters and I don't understand how to play them. I just want pvp that I don't have to wait 20 minutes for everytime I load in. So if you are someone like me that likes every fps genre except for extraction shooters, stay away from this one. It is not any different from the rest and it will not change your mind.

Game runs fine for me and I haven't experienced any issues so I'm not sure what's up with other people's systems. Aside from that though, I want to give an honest review that isn't just somebody crying that Marathon killed Destiny or an Arc Raiders glazer having nothing to say besides "game sucks". Anyways, I enjoy the game's concept, but it needs quite some work for it to be actually worth recommending.     What I DID NOT enjoy:  - To start, the menu is absolutely horrendous. I managed to somewhat understand how to navigate it, but it genuinely is just way too confusing.   - Second, there is hardly any sort of tutorial for the game to introduce you to playing. It gives you a very brief overview of some of the menus but that's about it. If I was someone who had never touched an extraction shooter before, I would refund assuming I bought the game.   - My third issue is that the AI is way too strong. 1-2 bullets will take out your basic shields. On top of that, if you get stuck having to reload while fighting the AI, they will just surround you and overpower you.  - Fourth, for whatever reason, most ammos have extremely little stacks which in turn means you have hardly any space to hold onto loot along with consumables. The ammo used in ARs and LMGs is 30 bullets per stack. Volt ammo used in pistols is 3 bullets per stack.   - Fifth, you can sprint for a total of like 10 seconds. In Marathon you don't have Stamina, but instead you have Heat and if the meter fills you begin to overheat making you unable to sprint or slide until you cool down. My issue with it though is that the bar fills up extremely fast especially for how slow you actually sprint. Sliding alone takes up about 1/3 of the heat bar.  - Sixth, where are the other players..? I played a handful of matches and I only ever saw 2 people and 1 looted building.     What I DID ENJOY:   - Everyone likes to badmouth the art style, but I kinda like it. It's extremely minimalistic but also futuristic as well. It's just something I haven't seen before personally.  - The guns feel solid. Idrk what else to say there. Only criticism is maybe the ttk is too fast for an extraction shooter and also given how little movement you get without using abilities.

This game is busy and extremely hard to understand. I've played for two hours and still have no idea how to play the game, or what is happening in the story. Nothing has been fun so far, I've died inexplicably, lost items that I still don't understand how to use, and when trying to play something past the solo beginner mode, immediately was killed by someone named Gabe. Was this a player? It was my first run on the map and first time matchmaking. I waited like 5 solid minutes for this to load, only to have no idea what happened 2 minutes into the match.  I know folks who like this game, but I stuck it out on my own after launching it and realizing that the worldbuilding was inexplicably terrible. The systems were confusing and so plentiful it made my head spin. On it's face, this probably *could* be a decent game. The overcomplexity makes zero sense at this point in my playthrough. It's an extraction shooter, I shouldn't be staring at a screen for 10 minutes in utter confusion regarding the skill trees, shells, inventory, skins, contracts, factions, ♥♥♥♥ ahhh please stop forcing me to look at screen after screen of walls of text that appear to be trying to contextualize the game. JUST SHOW IT THROUGH THE GAME. If your extraction shooter needs a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ manual to operate then it's too complex.   Bite sized reinforcement matters? I shouldn't be having to say this. I have not felt rewarded for anything that I've done in this game so far. I feel like I've been offered a dry and salty whole foods brand nutrient brick the size of my head and been told that if I finish the whoooooooole thing, I'll get a sweet treat in the midgame. Yeah nah I'm just gonna go do something else with my time. This game has tried to sell me an IOU for 2 hours, and I'm just gonna go skate instead.  I don't know what to say if I'm being honest. This is one of the worst games I've played in a really long time. I can't really go out of my way at this point to play it with my friend and learn what the hell, out of all of this mess, I need to actually be concerned with. As a new player I shouldn't feel like I just stared at static for two hours.

I think I didn't actually get out of the tutorial area and just died there repeatedly 4 or 5 times before uninstalling. In that time there was nothing compelling or satisfying - just a lot or dayglow 120% color saturated poster paints on all the surfaces - definitely designed for romper-room children (I felt).  I remember my 1st time in Apex Legends when the game was only a week or so old, I could survive to the end and I think I was only 2nd to the last to die.
